59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve $C$ over $\bar k$ of genus $g = g(C) > 0$ (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a $\bar k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$ to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component $\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}$ is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible $\bar k$-group scheme of dimension $g$; we write
73	$J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$ and let $\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J$ denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen $\mathbf 1$-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	$J$.
77	
78	The universal $T$-points of $J$ are degree-$0$ line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T$, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from $T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism $\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J$ of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A$ corresponding to a pointed morphism $\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	$(P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)$ defines a
87	regular morphism $\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$, the
88	birational morphism $\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J$ inverts to a
89	rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$, and the rational-map extension theorem
90	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
91	desired regular morphism $\psi$.
92	
93	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
94	
95	\begin{theorem}
96	[Albanese universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
97	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
98	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property}
99	  \uses{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism, def:symmetric_power_curve,
100	    lem:symmetric_product_av_map, lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian,
101	    lem:descent_through_birational_sigma,
102	    thm:rational_map_to_av_extends, def:pic_scheme}
103	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
104	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
105	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
106	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let $P$ be a $k$-rational point on $C$.  The map
107	  %  $f^P\colon C\,\to\,J$ has the following universal property: for any
108	  %  map $\varphi\colon C\to A$ from $C$ into an abelian variety sending
109	  %  $P$ to $0$, there is a unique homomorphism $\psi\colon J\to A$ such
110	  %  that $\varphi=\psi\circ f^P$."
111	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
112	
113	  Let $C$ be a complete nonsingular curve of genus $g > 0$ over an algebraically
114	  closed field $\bar k$, let $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$ be a $\bar k$-rational point,
115	  and write $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$, $\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J$ for the
116	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
117	  abelian variety $A$ over $\bar k$ and every pointed morphism
118	  $\varphi \colon C \to A$ with $\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A$, there exists a unique
119	  homomorphism of group schemes $\psi \colon J \to A$ such that
120	  $\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}$.
121	\end{theorem}
122	
123	The rest of this chapter develops the sub-lemmas
124	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}, \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
125	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}, \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
126	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
127	then gives the proof.
128	
129	\section{The Abel--Jacobi morphism at a marked point}
130	\label{sec:albaneseup_abel_jacobi}
131	
132	The first sub-lemma packages the construction and the elementary regularity of
133	the Abel--Jacobi morphism $\iota_{P_0}$ at the basepoint $P_0$. It is the
134	content gated on A.3 (existence of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ as a representable group
135	scheme).
136	
137	\begin{lemma}[Abel--Jacobi morphism at a basepoint]
138	  \label{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}
139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}
140	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, def:line_bundle_on_product}
141	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, the canonical map
142	  % $f^P\colon C\to J$ (introduction to Proposition~6.1), doc p.~104
143	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
145	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let $P$ be a $k$-rational point on $C$.  The map
146	  %  $f^P\colon C\to J$ has the following universal property: for any map
147	  %  $\varphi\colon C\to A$ from $C$ into an abelian variety sending $P$
148	  %  to $0$, there is a unique homomorphism $\psi\colon J\to A$ such that
149	  %  $\varphi=\psi\circ f^P$."
150	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, Summary 6.11, doc p.~107
151	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 113).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
153	  % "SUMMARY 6.11.  Between $(C,P)$ and itself, there is a divisorial
154	  %  correspondence $\mathcal L^P = \mathcal L(\Delta-\{P\}\times C-C\times\{P\})$.
155	  %    Between $(C,P)$ and $J$ there is the divisorial correspondence
156	  %  $\mathcal M^P$; for any divisorial correspondence $\mathcal L$ between
157	  %  $(C,P)$ and a pointed $k$-scheme $(T,t)$, there is a unique morphism
158	  %  of pointed $k$-schemes $\varphi\colon T\to J$ such that
159	  %  $(1\times \varphi)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L$.  In particular,
160	  %  there is a unique map $f^P\colon C\to J$ such that
161	  %  $(1\times f^P)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L^P$ and $f(P)=0$."
162	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1 and
163	  Summary~6.11, \S III.6.}
164	
165	  Let $C$ be a complete nonsingular curve of genus $g > 0$ over $\bar k$, let
166	  $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$ be a basepoint, and write $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$. There is a
167	  unique regular morphism of $\bar k$-schemes
168	  \[
169	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J, \qquad
170	    Q \;\longmapsto\; \bigl[\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\bigr],
171	  \]
172	  sending the basepoint $P_0$ to the identity element $\eta_J \in J(\bar k)$
173	  and characterised on $T$-points by the formula
174	  $(1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}$, where
175	  $\mathcal L^{P_0} := \mathcal O_{C \times C}\bigl(\Delta - \{P_0\} \times C -
176	  C \times \{P_0\}\bigr)$ is the rigidified diagonal correspondence on
177	  $C \times C$ and $\mathcal M^{P_0}$ is the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence
178	  on $C \times J$.
179	\end{lemma}
180	
181	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (no separate verbatim source; the existence of
182	% $f^P$ is part of Milne's standing setup at the head of \S III.6 -- ``$J$
183	% will be its Jacobian variety'' -- and is the moduli-of-degree-0-line-bundles
184	% interpretation of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ packaged into Summary~6.11.)
185	\begin{proof}
186	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, def:line_bundle_on_product}
187	  By A.3, $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$ is the moduli scheme of degree-zero line bundles
188	  on $C$; concretely, for every $\bar k$-scheme $T$ its $T$-points are
189	  isomorphism classes of line bundles $\mathcal L$ on $C \times_{\bar k} T$
190	  whose fibre degree at every geometric point of $T$ is zero, taken modulo line
191	  bundles pulled back from $T$ (the universal property of the Picard scheme
192	  packaged in \cref{def:pic_scheme}; the identity component imposes the
193	  degree-zero condition).
194	
195	  Apply this to $T := C$ and the line bundle
196	  \[
197	    \mathcal L^{P_0} \;:=\; \mathcal O_{C \times_{\bar k} C}\bigl(\Delta -
198	      \{P_0\} \times C - C \times \{P_0\}\bigr),
199	  \]
200	  the rigidified diagonal correspondence on $C \times_{\bar k} C$. Its fibre
201	  over a $\bar k$-point $Q \in C(\bar k)$ along the second projection is the
202	  degree-zero line bundle $\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)$ on $C$, so $\mathcal L^{P_0}$
203	  is a relative degree-zero line bundle, hence a $C$-point of $J$. The
204	  representability of $J$ at $T = C$ produces the unique morphism
205	  \[
206	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J
207	  \]
208	  such that $(1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}$
209	  for the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence $\mathcal M^{P_0}$ on $C \times J$.
210	
211	  At the basepoint, restriction of $\mathcal L^{P_0}$ to $C \times \{P_0\}$
212	  gives $\mathcal O_C(P_0 - P_0) \cong \mathcal O_C$, the trivial line bundle,
213	  whose moduli class in $J(\bar k)$ is the identity element $\eta_J$. Hence
214	  $\iota_{P_0}(P_0) = \eta_J$. Uniqueness of $\iota_{P_0}$ is the moduli
215	  uniqueness clause of representability: any other morphism $\iota'_{P_0}$
216	  satisfying $(1 \times \iota'_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}$
217	  and the pointed condition agrees with $\iota_{P_0}$ on $T$-points for every
218	  $T$, hence equals $\iota_{P_0}$ by the Yoneda embedding.
219	\end{proof}
220	
221	\section{The $g$-th symmetric power $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$}
222	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g}
223	
224	The Albanese universal property is built from $C$ by taking the
225	$g$-th \emph{symmetric power} $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ -- the quotient of the
226	$g$-fold Cartesian power $C^g$ by the natural action of the symmetric group
227	$S_g$. We collect the definition here as the first sub-lemma block.
228	
229	\begin{definition}[Symmetric power of a curve]
230	  \label{def:symmetric_power_curve}
231	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}
232	  \uses{}
233	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~3.1, \S III.3,
234	  % doc p.~94 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 100).
235	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
236	  % "For any variety $V$, the symmetric group $S_r$ on $r$ letters acts on
237	  %  the product of $r$ copies $V^r$ of $V$ by permuting the factors, and
238	  %  we want to define the $r$th symmetric power $V^{(r)}$ of $V$ to be the
239	  %  quotient $S_r \backslash V^r$.  The next proposition demonstrates the
240	  %  existence of $V^{(r)}$ and lists its main properties.
241	  %    A morphism $\varphi\colon V^r\to T$ is said to be {\bf symmetric} if
242	  %  $\varphi\sigma=\varphi$ for all $\sigma$ in $S_r$.
243	  %  PROPOSITION 3.1. Let $V$ be a variety over $k$.  Then there exists a
244	  %  variety $V^{(r)}$ and a symmetric morphism $\pi\colon V^r\to V^{(r)}$
245	  %  having the following properties:
246	  %    (a) as a topological space, $(V^{(r)},\pi)$ is the quotient of $V^r$
247	  %        by $S_r$;
248	  %    (b) for any open affine subset $U$ of $V$, $U^{(r)}$ is an open
249	  %        affine subset of $V^{(r)}$ and
250	  %             $\Gamma(U^{(r)},\mathcal O_{V^{(r)}}) = \Gamma(U^r,
251	  %             \mathcal O_{V^r})^{S_r}$
252	  %        (set of elements fixed by the action of $S_r$).
253	  %  The pair $(V^{(r)},\pi)$ has the following universal property: every
254	  %  symmetric $k$-morphism $\varphi\colon V^r\to T$ factors uniquely
255	  %  through $\pi$.
256	  %  The map $\pi$ is finite, surjective, and separable."
257	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~3.1, \S III.3, p.~94.}
258	
259	  Let $C$ be a complete nonsingular curve over an algebraically closed field
260	  $\bar k$ and let $g \ge 1$. The \emph{$g$-th symmetric power}
261	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ is a $\bar k$-scheme equipped with a finite surjective
262	  symmetric morphism $\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C$ characterised by
263	  the following universal property: for every $\bar k$-scheme $T$ and every
264	  symmetric $\bar k$-morphism $\varphi \colon C^g \to T$ (i.e.\ a morphism
265	  satisfying $\varphi \circ \sigma = \varphi$ for all $\sigma \in S_g$
266	  acting by permutation of the factors of $C^g$) there exists a unique
267	  morphism $\overline\varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to T$ with
268	  $\overline\varphi \circ \pi = \varphi$. As a topological space
269	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ is the quotient of $C^g$ by $S_g$, and for every open
270	  affine $U \subseteq C$ the open subset $U^{(g)} \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g
271	  C$ is affine with global sections the $S_g$-invariants
272	  $\Gamma(U^g, \mathcal O_{C^g})^{S_g}$.
273	\end{definition}
274	
275	\medskip
276	
277	\textbf{Mathlib status.} Mathlib has no formalised $g$-th symmetric power of
278	a scheme: only \texttt{Sym}/\texttt{SymmetricPower} for types and modules.
279	The corresponding scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied
280	project-side. The intended Lean construction follows Milne's recipe of
281	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}: on affine open $U = \Spec A \subseteq C$
282	form $\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}$, then glue via the standard affine
283	patching for an open cover $C = \bigcup U_i$. This sub-build is a
284	non-trivial dependency of A.4.d that the prover lane must absorb before the
285	main universal property closes; a \texttt{\textbackslash notready} marker is
286	appropriate on the corresponding Lean target until the construction lands.
287	For the proof of Proposition~3.1 itself, Milne refers to Mumford 1970,
288	\S II~\S 7, p.~66 and \S III \S 11, p.~112, for the patching details (the
289	quoted reference at the end of Milne's Proof on \S III.3, p.~94).
290	
291	\section{The morphism $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$}
292	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_av_map}
293	
294	The second sub-lemma is the elementary fact that the symmetric assignment
295	$(P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)$ from
296	$C^g$ to $A$ descends through $\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C$. The
297	key point is that the addition morphism $A^g \to A$ is itself
298	$S_g$-symmetric because $A$ is commutative as a group scheme.
299	
300	\begin{lemma}[Symmetrisation of a curve-to-AV morphism]
301	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}
303	  \uses{def:symmetric_power_curve}
304	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
305	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
306	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
307	  % "PROOF.  Consider the map
308	  %     $(P_1,...,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.$
309	  %  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through $C^{(g)}$."
310	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
311	  \S III.6, p.~104.}
312	
313	  Let $\varphi \colon C \to A$ be a morphism into an abelian variety $A$
314	  over $\bar k$. The composition
315	  \[
316	    \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}
317	    \;\colon\; C^g \;\longrightarrow\; A^g \;\longrightarrow\; A,
318	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\; \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g),
319	  \]
320	  is $S_g$-equivariant (where $S_g$ acts on $C^g$ by permutation of the
321	  factors and on $A$ trivially). Consequently, by the universal property of
322	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ (\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}), there is a unique
323	  morphism of $\bar k$-schemes
324	  \[
325	    \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A
326	  \]
327	  such that
328	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ \pi = \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}$.
329	\end{lemma}
330	
331	\begin{proof}
332	  \uses{def:symmetric_power_curve}
333	  Since $A$ is a commutative group scheme, the $g$-fold addition morphism
334	  $\mathrm{add}_A \colon A^g \to A$ satisfies $\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau =
335	  \mathrm{add}_A$ for every $\tau \in S_g$ permuting the factors of $A^g$
336	  (this is the scheme-theoretic statement that summation in a commutative
337	  monoid is independent of the order of summands; on $T$-points it is the
338	  identity $a_1 + \cdots + a_g = a_{\tau(1)} + \cdots + a_{\tau(g)}$ in the
339	  abelian group $A(T)$). The diagonal action of $\tau$ on $\varphi^{\times
340	  g} \colon C^g \to A^g$ is also given by permutation of factors. Therefore
341	  \[
342	    (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}) \circ \tau
343	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\varphi^{\times g} \circ \tau)
344	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\tau \circ \varphi^{\times g})
345	    \;=\; (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau) \circ \varphi^{\times g}
346	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g},
347	  \]
348	  so the composition $\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}$ is symmetric
349	  in the sense of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}. The universal property
350	  of $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ produces the unique factorisation
351	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$.
352	\end{proof}
353	
354	\section{The birational morphism $\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J$}
355	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_to_jacobian}
356	
357	The third sub-lemma is the birational identification of $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$
358	with $J$. After choosing the basepoint $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$, the cycle map
359	$(P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]$
360	defines a morphism $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$ which is
361	birational by Milne III~Theorem~5.1(a).
362	
363	\begin{lemma}[Birational morphism from $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ to $J$]
364	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}
365	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}
366	  \uses{def:symmetric_power_curve, lem:abel_jacobi_morphism, def:pic_scheme}
367	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~5.1(a), \S III.5,
368	  % doc p.~101 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 107).
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
370	  % "Let $f^r$ be the map $C^r\to J$ sending $(P_1,...,P_r)$ to
371	  %  $f(P_1)+\cdots+f(P_r)$.  On points, $f^r$ is the map
372	  %  $(P_1,...,P_r)\mapsto[P_1+\cdots+P_r-rP]$.  Clearly it is symmetric,
373	  %  and so induces a map $f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to J$.  We can regard
374	  %  $f^{(r)}$ as being the map sending an effective divisor $D$ of degree
375	  %  $r$ on $C$ to the linear equivalence class of $D-rP$.  The fibre of
376	  %  the map $f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}(k)\to J(k)$ containing $D$ can be
377	  %  identified with the space of effective divisors linearly equivalent
378	  %  to $D$, that is, with the linear system $|D|$.  The image of $C^{(r)}$
379	  %  in $J$ is a closed subvariety $W^r$ of $J$, which can also be written
380	  %  $W^r=f(C)+\cdots+f(C)$ ($r$ summands).
381	  %  THEOREM 5.1.  (a) For all $r\le g$, the morphism
382	  %  $f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to W^r$ is birational; in particular,
383	  %  $f^{(g)}$ is a birational map from $C^{(g)}$ onto $J$."
384	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~5.1(a) and the
385	  preceding paragraph, \S III.5, p.~101.}
386	
387	  Let $C$ be a complete nonsingular curve of genus $g > 0$ over $\bar k$,
388	  let $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$ be a basepoint, and write $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$,
389	  $\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J$ for the Abel--Jacobi morphism of
390	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The regular morphism
391	  $\iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J^g$ composed with the $g$-fold
392	  group-law addition $\mathrm{add}_J \colon J^g \to J$ descends through
393	  $\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C$ to a regular morphism
394	  \[
395	    f^{(g)} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; J,
396	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\;
397	      \bigl[\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)\bigr].
398	  \]
399	  The morphism $f^{(g)}$ is birational: it is dominant (its image is
400	  $J$, the $g$-fold sum $\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)$ exhausts
401	  $J$ since $\dim J = g$ and the iterated sum reaches full dimension), and
402	  its generic fibre is a single $\bar k$-point (a degree-$g$ effective
403	  divisor $D$ on $C$ with $h^0(D) = 1$ is the unique element of its linear
404	  system $|D|$; such divisors form a dense open subset of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
405	  C$ -- a consequence of $\dim |D| = 0$ generically on a curve of genus $g$
406	  by Riemann--Roch). Equivalently, $f^{(g)}$ is a birational morphism in
407	  the sense that there exist dense opens
408	  $U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C$, $V \subseteq J$ such that
409	  $f^{(g)}|_U \colon U \to V$ is an isomorphism.
410	\end{lemma}
411	
412	\begin{proof}
413	  \uses{def:symmetric_power_curve, lem:abel_jacobi_morphism, def:pic_scheme}
414	  Symmetry of $\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g}$ is the
415	  specialisation of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} to the abelian
416	  variety $A := J$ and the morphism $\varphi := \iota_{P_0}$; the universal
417	  property of $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ produces the regular morphism $f^{(g)}$.
418	  The closed-form description on $\bar k$-points,
419	  $f^{(g)}(P_1, \ldots, P_g) = [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]$,
420	  follows because $\iota_{P_0}(P_i) = [\mathcal O_C(P_i - P_0)]$ by
421	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} and the group law on $J$ is the
422	  tensor-product of degree-zero line bundles.
423	
424	  We verify birationality. The image of $f^{(g)}$ in $J$ is the iterated
425	  sum $\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)$ ($g$ summands), which is
426	  closed (it is the image of the proper morphism
427	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$, $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ being proper because $C^g$
428	  is proper and $\pi$ is finite surjective). By the dimension count
429	  $\dim \mathrm{Sym}^g C = g = \dim J$ (Riemann--Roch and the standing
430	  hypothesis $g > 0$), the image $W^g \subseteq J$ has dimension at most
431	  $g$, and equality forces $W^g = J$ since $J$ is irreducible. Hence
432	  $f^{(g)}$ is surjective with $\dim$-equal source and target.
433	
434	  For the generic-fibre identification, Riemann--Roch
435	  $h^0(D) - h^1(D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ at $\deg D = g$ gives $h^0(D) - h^1(D)
436	  = 1$, so for a general $D \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)$ with $h^1(D) =
437	  0$ (the locus where $h^1$ jumps is closed of positive codimension on a
438	  smooth curve, by upper semicontinuity of cohomology), we have $h^0(D) =
439	  1$ and the linear system $|D|$ consists of $D$ alone. Therefore the
440	  fibre of $f^{(g)}$ over the generic point of $J$ contains a single point
441	  $D$, i.e.\ $f^{(g)}$ is birational onto $J$.
442	
443	  By \cref{def:pic_scheme} the underlying $\bar k$-scheme $J$ is the
444	  Picard scheme $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ obtained at A.3, so the moduli-theoretic
445	  reading of $f^{(g)}$ (effective divisor class $\mapsto$ degree-zero
446	  line bundle class via $D \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(D - g P_0)]$) is the
447	  same as the Yoneda image of the $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$-point
448	  $[\mathcal O_{C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g C}(\mathcal D_g - g\{P_0\} \times
449	  \mathrm{Sym}^g C)]$, where $\mathcal D_g \subseteq C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g
450	  C$ is the universal effective Cartier divisor of degree $g$ on $C$. We
451	  do not need this Yoneda repackaging for the proof of the Albanese UP --
452	  the symmetric-power route uses only the morphism $f^{(g)}$ and its
453	  birationality.
454	\end{proof}
455	
456	\section{Descent through the birational quotient $\sigma$}
457	\label{sec:albaneseup_descent}
458	
459	The final auxiliary lemma packages the two-step descent that promotes a
460	morphism on $C^g$ (after passing through $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ via
461	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and inverting $f^{(g)}$ via
462	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}) to a regular morphism on
463	$J$. The output is a rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$, which is then
464	upgraded to a regular morphism via Milne's Theorem~3.2
465	(\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}). This is the unique step of the
466	proof that consumes A.4.c.
467	
468	\begin{lemma}[Descent of $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi$ to a regular morphism $J \to A$]
469	  \label{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}
470	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}
471	  \uses{lem:symmetric_product_av_map, lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian,
472	    thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
473	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
474	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
475	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
476	  % "It therefore defines a rational map $\psi\colon J\to A$, which (I 3.2)
477	  %  shows to be a regular map."
478	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
479	  \S III.6, p.~104 (the sentence ``which (I~3.2) shows to be a regular
480	  map'').}
481	
482	  Let $\varphi \colon C \to A$ be a morphism into an abelian variety $A$
483	  over $\bar k$. Let $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$
484	  be the symmetrised morphism of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and
485	  $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$ the birational morphism of
486	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}. Then there is a unique
487	  regular morphism
488	  \[
489	    \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A
490	  \]
491	  such that $\psi \circ f^{(g)} = \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi$ on the dense open
492	  on which $f^{(g)}$ is an isomorphism (equivalently, $\psi$ is the unique
493	  regular morphism extending the rational map
494	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1} \colon J \dashrightarrow
495	  A$).
496	\end{lemma}
497	
498	\begin{proof}
499	  \uses{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian, thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
500	  By \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, there exist dense opens
501	  $U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C$ and $V \subseteq J$ with $f^{(g)}|_U \colon
502	  U \xrightarrow{\sim} V$ an isomorphism. Setting $\psi_0 \colon V \to A$
503	  to be the composition $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi|_U \circ (f^{(g)}|_U)^{-1}
504	  \colon V \to U \to A$ produces a rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$.
505	
506	  The target $A$ is an abelian variety and the source $J$ is smooth over
507	  $\bar k$ (it is itself an abelian variety, hence nonsingular). Milne's
508	  Theorem~3.2 (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) therefore promotes
509	  $\psi_0$ to a unique regular morphism $\psi \colon J \to A$ extending
510	  $\psi_0$ on $V$. Uniqueness of $\psi$ follows from the
511	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle: two regular morphisms
512	  $J \to A$ agreeing on a dense open are equal.
513	\end{proof}
514	
515	\section{Proof of the universal property}
516	\label{sec:albaneseup_proof}
517	
518	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
519	% "PROOF.  Consider the map
520	%    $$(P_1,\ldots,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.$$
521	%  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through $C^{(g)}$.  It
522	%  therefore defines a rational map $\psi\colon J\to A$, which (I 3.2)
523	%  shows to be a regular map.  It is clear from the construction that
524	%  $\psi\circ f^P=\varphi$ (note that $f^P$ is the composite of $Q\mapsto
525	%  Q+(g-1)P\colon C\to C^{(g)}$ with $f^{(g)}\colon C^{(g)}\to J$).  In
526	%  particular, $\psi$ maps 0 to 0, and (I 1.2) shows that it is therefore
527	%  a homomorphism.  If $\psi'$ is a second homomorphism such that
528	%  $\psi'\circ f^P=\varphi$, then $\psi$ and $\psi'$ agree on
529	%  $f^P(C)+\cdots+f^P(C)$ ($g$ copies), which is the whole of $J$."
530	\begin{proof}[Proof of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}]
531	  \uses{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism, def:symmetric_power_curve,
532	    lem:symmetric_product_av_map, lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian,
533	    lem:descent_through_birational_sigma,
534	    thm:rational_map_to_av_extends, def:pic_scheme,
535	    thm:rigidity_lemma}
536	  The proof follows Milne's Proposition~6.1 verbatim, using the four
537	  sub-lemmas of this chapter:
538	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} (Abel--Jacobi morphism $\iota_{P_0}$),
539	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} (symmetrisation of $\varphi$ to
540	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$),
541	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian} (birational morphism
542	  $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$), and
543	  \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} (descent of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
544	  \varphi$ to a regular morphism $\psi \colon J \to A$ via
545	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}).
546	  \begin{enumerate}
547	    \item \emph{Symmetrisation.} Consider the map $C^g \to A$ sending
548	      $(P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)$
549	      (Milne's typeset proof writes $\sum_i \psi(P_i)$ in this line, but
550	      the construction is the symmetrisation of the input $\varphi$; we
551	      use the unambiguous symbol $\varphi$ throughout). Since $A$ is
552	      commutative this map is $S_g$-symmetric. By
553	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} it factors through the
554	      symmetric power $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$, defining a regular morphism
555	      \[
556	        \widetilde \psi \;:=\; \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi
557	        \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A.
558	      \]
559	    \item \emph{Descent to a regular morphism on $J$.} By
560	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, the morphism
561	      $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$ is birational. The
562	      composite $\widetilde \psi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1}$ is, a priori,
563	      only a rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$. By
564	      \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} -- whose proof is an
565	      application of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne
566	      Theorem~I~3.2, rational map from a nonsingular variety to an
567	      abelian variety is regular) -- this rational map extends uniquely
568	      to a regular morphism
569	      \[
570	        \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A.
571	      \]
572	    \item \emph{Factorisation $\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi$.}
573	      Milne's identification: $\iota_{P_0}$ is the composite
574	      \[
575	        C \;\xrightarrow{\;Q \mapsto Q + (g-1) P_0\;}\;
576	        \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\xrightarrow{\;f^{(g)}\;}\; J,
577	      \]
578	      where the first arrow sends a point $Q \in C(\bar k)$ to the
579	      effective divisor $Q + (g-1) P_0 \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)$
580	      (its image under $f^{(g)}$ in $J$ is
581	      $[\mathcal O_C(Q + (g-1) P_0 - g P_0)] = [\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)]
582	      = \iota_{P_0}(Q)$, matching \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}). On
583	      $\bar k$-points,
584	      \[
585	        \psi(\iota_{P_0}(Q)) = \widetilde\psi(Q + (g-1) P_0)
586	          = \varphi(Q) + (g-1)\varphi(P_0) = \varphi(Q),
587	      \]
588	      using $\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A$ and that $\eta_A$ is the identity of
589	      $A$. Hence $\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi$ on $\bar k$-points,
590	      and therefore $\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi$ as morphisms of
591	      $\bar k$-schemes (both sides are regular morphisms $C \to A$ from
592	      a reduced separated scheme to a separated scheme, agreeing on
593	      dense $\bar k$-points, hence agreeing everywhere by the
594	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle).
595	    \item \emph{Homomorphism property.} The morphism $\psi$ sends
596	      $\eta_J$ to $\eta_A$: indeed,
597	      $\psi(\eta_J) = \psi(\iota_{P_0}(P_0)) = \varphi(P_0) = \eta_A$.
598	      A regular morphism of abelian varieties sending the identity to
599	      the identity is a homomorphism of group schemes, by Milne
600	      Corollary~I~1.2 (a consequence of the Rigidity Lemma
601	      \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, see
602	      \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, Corollary~1.2). Therefore
603	      $\psi$ is a homomorphism of group schemes $J \to A$.
604	    \item \emph{Uniqueness.} Suppose $\psi'$ is a second homomorphism
605	      with $\psi' \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi$. Then $\psi$ and
606	      $\psi'$ agree on the set-theoretic image $\iota_{P_0}(C) \subseteq
607	      J$. Both are homomorphisms, so they agree on the subgroup
608	      generated by $\iota_{P_0}(C)$ -- which contains the $g$-fold sum
609	      $\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)$ ($g$ copies). The
610	      $g$-fold sum is the image of
611	      $\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J$,
612	      which descends to $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$, which
613	      is surjective by \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}.
614	      Therefore $\psi$ and $\psi'$ agree on a dense open subset of $J$
615	      (in fact on all of $J$ as a set), hence everywhere by the
616	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle.
617	  \end{enumerate}
618	  This completes the proof.
619	\end{proof}
620	
621	\section{Wiring for the positive-genus Albanese witness}
622	\label{sec:albaneseup_witness_wiring}
623	
624	\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} supplies the universal-factorisation
625	field $\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}$ of an Albanese witness for $(C, P_0)$ in the
626	sense of \cref{def:JacobianWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}); the remaining
627	fields are supplied by A.3:
628	\begin{itemize}
629	  \item $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$ (the underlying scheme).
630	  \item $\texttt{grpObj}$: the group-object structure on $J$ obtained from the
631	    abelian-group structure on $\Pic^0_{C/k}(T)$ for varying $T$, refined to a
632	    group object in the category of $\bar k$-schemes (A.3, A.2.c group-scheme
633	    refinement).
634	  \item $\texttt{proper}$: properness of $\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec \bar k$ from
635	    the closed-immersion property of the identity component in the proper
636	    representing Picard scheme (A.3; cf.\
637	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_proper}'s discussion).
638	  \item $\texttt{smoothGenus}$: smoothness of relative dimension $g(C)$ from
639	    the deformation-theoretic tangent-space identification with $H^1(C,
640	    \mathcal O_C)$, which has dimension $g(C)$ by Riemann--Roch.
641	  \item $\texttt{geomIrred}$: geometric irreducibility of the identity
642	    component of a $\bar k$-group scheme locally of finite type (Kleiman
643	    Lem.~lem:agps and Prp.~prp:pic0; cf.\
644	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}'s discussion).
645	  \item $\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}$: the present
646	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}, applied at each
647	    $P_0 \in C(\bar k)$.
648	\end{itemize}
649	
650	The witness so produced is over $\bar k$. The Galois-descent step from $\bar
651	k$ back to the original base field $k$ -- packaged at
652	\cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f -- closes the loop and supplies the
653	$k$-side Albanese witness consumed by \cref{def:Jacobian}.
654	
655	\section{Lean encoding}
656	\label{sec:albaneseup_lean_encoding}
657	
658	The Lean target file is the new
659	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean} (declared at the top of
660	this chapter). The five target declarations are:
661	\begin{itemize}
662	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}: the regular morphism
663	    $\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}$ of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}.
664	    The signature exposes the marked point $P_0$ as an explicit argument and
665	    the Picard-scheme construction $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ as an existing object.
666	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}: the scheme-theoretic
667	    $g$-th symmetric power $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$ of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
668	    packaged with the canonical projection
669	    $\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C$ and its universal property
670	    (factorisation of every $S_g$-symmetric morphism out of $C^g$). \emph{This
671	    declaration has no Mathlib analogue}: Mathlib's \texttt{Sym} /
672	    \texttt{SymmetricPower} cover types and modules only; the
673	    scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied project-side, following
674	    Milne's affine-and-glue recipe of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}.
675	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}: the
676	    symmetrised morphism
677	    $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A$ produced by
678	    \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} from an input morphism
679	    $\varphi \colon C \to A$ into an abelian variety.
680	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}: the
681	    birational morphism $f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J$ of
682	    \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, together with the data of a
683	    dense open isomorphism $U \xrightarrow{\sim} V$ realising
684	    birationality. The signature exposes the basepoint $P_0$ and consumes
685	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}.
686	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}: the
687	    descent lemma of \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma},
688	    consuming \texttt{symmetricPowerAVMap} and
689	    \texttt{symmetricPowerToJacobian} together with
690	    \texttt{Albanese.Thm32RationalMapExtension.extend\_to\_av}
691	    (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) to produce the regular
692	    morphism $\psi \colon J \to A$.
693	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese\_universal\_property}: the
694	    main theorem, the existence-and-uniqueness statement of
695	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}. The signature is
696	    \[
697	      \texttt{P\_0 : C(\bar k)} \;\to\;
698	      \texttt{A : AbelianVariety $\bar k$} \;\to\;
699	      (\varphi : C \to A) \;\to\;
700	      (h\varphi : \varphi \circ P_0 = \eta_A) \;\to\;
701	      \exists ! \, \psi \colon J \to A, \;\; \psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi
702	      \;\wedge\; \psi \text{ is a group homomorphism}.
703	    \]
704	\end{itemize}
705	
706	The proof body of \texttt{albanese\_universal\_property} consumes its four
707	sub-lemma exports plus \texttt{Albanese.Thm32RationalMapExtension.extend\_to\_av}
708	(\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) and Milne's Corollary~I~1.2
709	(\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} consequence, registered at
710	\cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}); the wiring follows the five-step proof
711	of \S\ref{sec:albaneseup_proof}.
712	
713	The Picard-scheme inputs (\cref{def:pic_scheme} of
714	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) are imported from
715	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean} (when that file
716	materialises along the A.2.c row) and the identity-component / degree-map
717	refinement is gated on the A.3 row (which does not yet have a dedicated
718	chapter; see ``Notes for Plan Agent'' below). At the time of writing, the
719	prover-side declaration body
720	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albane\-se\_universal\_property} should be
721	skeletoned as a \texttt{sorry} that consumes the A.3 instances as
722	hypotheses with named placeholders, and \texttt{Sym\-met\-ric\-Power} should
723	be skeletoned with a \texttt{notready} marker until the affine-and-glue
724	sub-build lands. The remaining sub-lemma bodies become routine once both
725	inputs are available.
726	
727	\section{Out of scope}
728	\label{sec:albaneseup_out_of_scope}
729	
730	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Albanese universal property
731	(\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} = Milne Proposition~III~6.1) and the
732	four internal sub-lemmas of the symmetric-power proof
733	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism},
734	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
735	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map},
736	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
737	\cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}), plus the wiring of these into
738	a positive-genus Albanese witness. The following adjacent content is
739	explicitly out of scope:
740	\begin{itemize}
741	  \item \textbf{Representability of $\Pic_{C/k}$ (A.2.c).} Proved in
742	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} as
743	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}; this chapter only consumes it.
744	  \item \textbf{The identity component $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ and the degree map
745	    (A.3).} The identity component refinement of the Picard scheme together
746	    with its degree map is the subject of a forthcoming chapter; this chapter
747	    consumes $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ as a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
748	    $\bar k$-group scheme of dimension $g$ but does not construct it.
749	  \item \textbf{Milne's Theorem~3.2 (A.4.c).} Proved in
750	    \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
751	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only consumes it
752	    inside \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} to promote the
753	    rational map $J \dashrightarrow A$ to a regular morphism.
754	  \item \textbf{The scheme-theoretic construction of $\mathrm{Sym}^g C$.}
755	    Mathlib has no $g$-th symmetric power of a scheme; the
756	    \texttt{SymmetricPower} target of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve} is a
757	    project-side sub-build (affine $S_g$-invariants
758	    $\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}$ on each open affine $\Spec A \subseteq C$,
759	    glued by the standard open-cover patching of Milne~III.3
760	    Proposition~3.1 / Mumford~1970 II.7 \& III.11). The construction itself
761	    is a non-trivial dependency of A.4.d; this chapter \emph{specifies} the
762	    target but does not develop its construction.
763	  \item \textbf{The autoduality $J \cong J^\vee$ (Milne~III \S 6.6) and the
764	    moduli / Poincar\'e-bundle Albanese route.} The iter-174 draft of this
765	    chapter proposed a route via the Poincar\'e bundle of $A$ and the
766	    autoduality of $J$, which gates on the theorem of the cube. The cube
767	    was excised from the project in iter-163; the autoduality / moduli
768	    route is therefore demoted and replaced by the symmetric-power route
769	    of this chapter. A reference recording of the moduli wording is given
770	    in the ``Alternative route history'' \texttt{\%~NOTE} block at the end
771	    of this file.
772	  \item \textbf{Milne Proposition~III~6.4 / Remark~6.5 / Theorem~6.6} (the
773	    divisorial-correspondence universal property for $C \times C \to A$,
774	    the identification of $(J, F)$ with the Albanese variety in the sense
775	    of Lang, and the autoduality $J \cong J^\vee$). Not needed by the
776	    symmetric-power route.
777	  \item \textbf{Weil's reconstruction of $J$ from the rational law of
778	    composition on $C^{(g)}$ (Milne~III \S 7).} The project does not need
779	    this alternative construction.
780	  \item \textbf{The genus-$0$ case.} The standing hypothesis of this chapter
781	    is $g(C) > 0$ (Milne's standing hypothesis of \S III.6). The genus-$0$
782	    case of the Albanese witness is supplied by
783	    \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}), routed through
784	    \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} and the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
785	    shortcut; that construction is independent of and disjoint from the
786	    Pic$^0$-route of this chapter.
787	  \item \textbf{Generalisations to a non-algebraically-closed base $k$.}
788	    The chapter works over $\bar k$ throughout. The descent of the witness
789	    back to $k$ via Galois descent of morphism equality is the subject of
790	    \cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f.
791	\end{itemize}

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve $C$ over $k$ (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set$ of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	$C/k$ projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	$\Hilb_{C/k}$ and its open subscheme $\Div_{C/k}$ of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective $k$-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for $\Pic$ assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map $\Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} $\Pic_{C/k}$. The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on $C \times_k T$ to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor $D \subset C \times_k T$ over $T$
64	(an $S$-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of $C \times_k T$ flat over $T$, hence a $T$-point of the Hilbert
66	functor $\Hilb_{C/k}(T)$, which is the special case $\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)$ on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$.
70	
71	\begin{lemma}[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
72	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
74	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, thm:pullback_natural}
75	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
76	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
77	  % divisors by an open subscheme of $\IHilb_{X/S}$); arXiv:math/0504020,
78	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
79	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
80	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
81	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
82	  %   Assume $X/S$ is projective and flat.  Then $\Div_{X/S}$ is
83	  %  representable by an open subscheme $\IDiv_{X/S}$ of the Hilbert scheme
84	  %  $\IHilb_{X/S}$.
85	  %   \end{thm}"
86	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
87	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
88	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
89	  %      $$A_{X/S}(T)\:\Div_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)$$
90	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor $D$ on $X_T/T$ to the
91	  %  sheaf $\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)$.  The target $\Pic_{X/S}$ may be replaced by any
92	  %  of its associated sheaves."
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
94	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
95	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where $\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}$).}
96	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
97	  $T$ be a $k$-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$
98	  \[
99	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \Div_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
100	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
101	  \]
102	  where $\Div_{C/k}(T)$ is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
103	  on $C \times_k T$ flat over $T$, and the target is the
104	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
105	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor $\Div_{C/k}$ is representable
106	  by an open subscheme $\Div_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}$ of the Hilbert scheme
107	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with $\Hilb_{C/k}$ the special case
108	  $\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
109	  representable by a morphism of $k$-schemes $A_{C/k} : \Div_{C/k} \to
110	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
114	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
115	% "Set $H:=\IHilb_{X/S}$, and let $W\subset X\x H$ be the universal
116	% (closed) subscheme, and $q\:W\to H$ the projection.  Let $V$ denote the
117	% set of points $w\in W$ at which $W$ is an effective divisor. Plainly $V$
118	% is open in $W$.  Set $Z:=q(W-V)$.  Then $Z$ is closed because $q$ is
119	% proper.  Set $U:=H-Z$.  Then $U$ is open, and $q^{-1}U$ is an effective
120	% divisor in $X\x U$.  In fact, since $q$ is flat, $q^{-1}U$ is a relative
121	% effective divisor in $X\x U/U$.
122	% [\dots]"
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, thm:pullback_natural}
125	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
126	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ is representable by a $k$-scheme; this uses
127	  $C/k$ projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
128	  stronger). Let $W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}$ be the universal
129	  closed subscheme and $q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}$ the projection. The locus
130	  $V \subseteq W$ where $W$ is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
131	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement $W \setminus V$
132	  is closed in $W$, and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
133	  \Hilb_{C/k}$ is closed because $q$ is proper. Hence $\Div_{C/k} :=
134	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z$ is open in $\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
135	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on $\Div_{C/k}$ the
136	  restriction of $W$ is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
137	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
138	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
139	  \Div_{C/k} / \Div_{C/k}$.
140	
141	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor $D \subseteq C \times_k T$
142	  to the invertible sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}$
143	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
144	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in $T$: pullback of an ideal
145	  sheaf along $\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T$
146	  takes the ideal of $D$ to the ideal of $D_{T'}$ (since $D$ is flat over
147	  $T$, the schematic inverse image $D_{T'}$ is a relative effective
148	  divisor on $C \times_k T'/T'$, again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
149	  takes $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)$ to $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})$.
150	  Composing with the canonical projection
151	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ of
152	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
153	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
154	  natural map $A_{C/k}(T) : \Div_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
158	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
159	
160	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
161	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
162	of Route~A: $S = \Spec k$, $X = C$ a smooth proper geometrically integral
163	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
164	$X/S$ projective Zariski-locally over $S$, flat, with integral geometric
165	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
166	
167	\begin{theorem}[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
168	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
169	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
170	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence,
171	        thm:quot_representable, def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
172	        thm:pullback_natural}
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to $S = \Spec k$, $X$ complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume $f\:X\to S$ is projective Zariski locally over $S$, and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over $S$, and represents $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$.
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also $S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective, then
186	  %  $\IPic_{X/S}$ is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  $S$-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$, and $X$ is complete.  Then
193	  %  $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists, represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}$, and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let $k$ be a field and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over $k$. Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a $k$-scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, separated and locally of
202	  finite type over $k$, which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective $k$-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of $C/k$.
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on $S$ to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of $S$-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on $S$ to prove that an $S$-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% $S$ is Noetherian and $X/S$ is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an $S$-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  \uses{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence, thm:quot_representable,
224	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:pullback_natural,
225	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
226	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to $S = \Spec k$ and
227	  $X = C$. The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
228	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
229	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
230	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
231	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
232	
233	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
234	  For each polynomial $\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]$, let $P^\phi \subseteq
235	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ be the \'etale subsheaf whose $T$-points are
236	  represented by invertible sheaves $\mathcal{L}$ on $C \times_k T$ with
237	  $\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)$ for all $t \in T$ (the
238	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
239	  $\mathcal{O}_C(1)$ on the projective curve $C$). The $P^\phi$ form a
240	  disjoint open cover of $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (openness via Kleiman's
241	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
242	  represent each $P^\phi$ by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
243	  $k$-schemes.
244	
245	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by $m$-regularity.}
246	  Given $\phi$, fix $m$ large enough that the $T$-points of $P^\phi$ are
247	  representable by $\mathcal{L}$ on $C \times_k T$ satisfying
248	  $R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0$ for all $i \ge 1$, $n \ge m$. Twisting
249	  by $\mathcal{O}_C(m)$ defines an automorphism of $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$
250	  that carries $P^\phi$ to $P^{\phi_0}_0$ for $\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)$.
251	  Hence it suffices to represent each $P^{\phi_0}_0$ by a quasi-projective
252	  $k$-scheme.
253	
254	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
255	  Consider the Abel map $A_{C/k} : \Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$
256	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source $\Div_{C/k}$
257	  is representable by an open subscheme of $\Hilb_{C/k}$ (and hence by an
258	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
259	  is quasi-projective over $k$. Pulling back along
260	  $P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ yields an open
261	  subscheme $Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \Div_{C/k}$
262	  of $\Div_{C/k}$, which is again quasi-projective. The projection
263	  $\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
264	  given a $T$-point $\lambda$ of $P^{\phi_0}_0$ represented by an \'etale
265	  cover $T' \to T$ and an invertible sheaf $\mathcal{L}'$ on $C \times_k
266	  T'$ with $H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
267	  $\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'$ associated to
268	  $\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'$ is smooth over $T'$; an \'etale
269	  cover $T_1 \to T'$ admits a $T'$-map $T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})$
270	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
271	  $\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z$ produces the required $T_1$-point of $Z$.
272	  Moreover, the first projection $Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z$ is
273	  smooth and proper.
274	
275	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
276	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection $\alpha : Z \to P$
277	  of \'etale sheaves with $Z$ quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$
278	  representable, and the first projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper,
279	  $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
280	  $P = P^{\phi_0}_0$, $Z$ and $R$ as above, so $P^{\phi_0}_0$ is
281	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over $\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]$ and
282	  over $m$, the disjoint union of representable $P^\phi$ pieces represents
283	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$. The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
284	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
285	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the $S = \Spec k$,
286	  $X = C$ complete case.
287	\end{proof}
288	
289	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
290	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
291	
292	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
293	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
294	abstract content allowing the descent from $Z = \Div^{\phi_0}_0$ to the
295	representing Picard piece.
296	
297	\begin{lemma}[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
298	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
299	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
300	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
301	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
302	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
303	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
304	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
305	  %   Let $\alpha\:Z\to P$ be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set $R:=Z\x_PZ$.
306	  %  Assume $\alpha$ is a surjection, $Z$ is representable by a
307	  %  quasi-projective $S$-scheme, $R$ is representable by an $S$-scheme, and
308	  %  the first projection $R\to Z$ is representable by a smooth and proper
309	  %  map.  Then $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective $S$-scheme, and
310	  %  $\alpha$ is representable by a smooth map."
311	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
312	  Let $\alpha : Z \to P$ be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)$.
313	  Set $R := Z \times_P Z$. Suppose:
314	  \begin{enumerate}
315	    \item $\alpha$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
316	    \item $Z$ is representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme;
317	    \item $R$ is representable by a $k$-scheme;
318	    \item the first projection $R \to Z$ is representable by a smooth
319	      and proper map.
320	  \end{enumerate}
321	  Then $P$ is representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme, and $\alpha$
322	  is representable by a smooth map.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
326	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
327	% structural heart of step 4.)
328	% "To lighten the notation, let $Z$ and $R$ denote the
329	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map $Z\to S$ is
330	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
331	% $Z\x_SZ\to Z$ is too.  But the first projection $R\to Z$ is proper, and
332	% factors naturally through a map $h\:R\to Z\x_SZ$.  Hence $h$ is proper.
333	% But $h$ is a monomorphism; that is, $h$ is injective on $T$-points.
334	% Therefore, $h$ is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
335	%
336	% Plainly, for each $S$-scheme $T$, the subset $R(T)\subset
337	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
338	% map of schemes $R\to Z$ is flat and proper, and $Z$ is a
339	% quasi-projective $S$-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
340	% quasi-projective $S$-scheme $Q$ and a faithfully flat and projective map
341	% $Z\to Q$ such that $R=Z\x_QZ$.  (In other words, a flat and proper
342	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
343	% [\dots]
344	% Since $Z\to Q$ is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
345	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
346	% same as those of $R\to Z$.  And $R\to Z$ is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
347	% $Z\to Q$ is smooth.
348	%
349	% It remains to see that $Q$ represents $P$.  First, observe that $Z\to Q$
350	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
351	% [\dots]
352	% Since $Z\to Q$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, $Q$ is, in the
353	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
354	% $R\rightrightarrows Z$ by Exercise~\ref{ex:epi} below, which is an
355	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
356	% exercise, $P$ too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
357	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus $Q$
358	% represents $P$."
359	\begin{proof}
360	  By (ii) the structure map $Z \to \Spec k$ is quasi-projective, hence
361	  separated, so the first projection $Z \times_k Z \to Z$ is separated.
362	  By (iv) the first projection $R \to Z$ is proper; it factors through
363	  $h : R \to Z \times_k Z$ over the diagonal embedding (the second
364	  component is the second projection of $R \to Z \times_P Z$). The map
365	  $h$ is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because $R$ is the graph
366	  of an equivalence relation on $Z$, hence injective on $T$-points).
367	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: $h$ is a
368	  closed embedding.
369	
370	  Hence $R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z$ is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
371	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Z$. By the
372	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
373	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
374	  the graph $R \subseteq Z \times_k Z$ inside the universal subscheme of
375	  the Hilbert scheme $\Hilb_{Z/k}$ and descends to a closed subscheme
376	  $Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}$ representing the quotient), there exist a
377	  quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Q$ and a faithfully flat, projective map
378	  $Z \to Q$ with $R = Z \times_Q Z$.
379	
380	  The map $Z \to Q$ is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
381	  extension, coincide with those of $R \to Z$, which are smooth by
382	  hypothesis. Hence $Z \to Q$ is smooth.
383	
384	  Finally, $Z \to Q$ is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
385	  cover of any $T$-point of $Q$ pulls back to a $T'$-point of $Z$, by
386	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to $Z \times_Q T \to T$). In the category
387	  of \'etale sheaves, both $Q$ and $P$ are coequalisers of the pair
388	  $R \rightrightarrows Z$; the coequaliser is unique up to unique
389	  isomorphism, so $Q$ represents $P$. Smoothness of $\alpha : Z \to P$
390	  is the smoothness of $Z \to Q$ just shown.
391	\end{proof}
392	
393	\section{Group-scheme structure}
394	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
395	
396	The Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ inherits an abelian group structure from
397	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on $C \times_k T$. The relative
398	Picard presheaf $T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T$ is already an abelian
399	group at every $T$ (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
400	functorially in $T$; this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
401	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
402	
403	\begin{theorem}[$\Pic_{C/k}$ is a $k$-group scheme]
404	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
405	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
406	  \uses{def:pic_scheme, thm:fga_pic_representability,
407	        def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
408	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
409	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
410	  % text (group-scheme structure on $\IPic_{X/S}$ via tensor product);
411	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
412	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
415	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
416	  %      $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
417	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
418	  %  topologies  by
419	  %      $$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
420	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
421	  %   \end{dfn}"
422	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
423	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
424	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~\ref{df:Pfs}
425	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
426	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by $\IPic_{X/S}$.  Moreover, we say simply that the
427	  %  Picard scheme $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists."
428	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
429	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
430	  Let $\Pic_{C/k}$ be the Picard scheme of
431	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then $\Pic_{C/k}$ is canonically an
432	  abelian group scheme over $k$: the tensor product
433	  $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]$
434	  and inverse $-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$ on
435	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$ (well defined because both operations
436	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
437	  $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
438	  are natural in $T$, hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
439	  $m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$,
440	  $i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$, and
441	  $e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}$ (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
442	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, $\Pic_{C/k}$ is a $k$-group
443	  scheme locally of finite type.
444	\end{theorem}
445	
446	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
447	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
448	% the fact that $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
449	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
450	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
451	% records the standard transfer.
452	\begin{proof}
453	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
454	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
455	  The relative Picard functor $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ of
456	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
457	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
458	  \mathcal{M}]$, inverse $-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
459	  $0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]$; these operations descend through
460	  the quotient by $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ because $\pi_T^*$ is itself a
461	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
462	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
463	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
464	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
465	  in the value category).
466	
467	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ is
468	  represented by the scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$. The Yoneda embedding is fully
469	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
470	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
471	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
472	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (inverse), and the
473	  unit map $h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ pick out unique
474	  morphisms of representing schemes $m$, $i$, $e$ as displayed. The
475	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
476	  inverse) hold at the level of $T$-points functorially in $T$, hence
477	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
478	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
479	\end{proof}
480	
481	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
482	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
483	
484	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
485	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
486	
487	\begin{definition}[The Picard scheme]
488	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
489	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
490	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability, def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
491	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
492	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
493	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
494	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
495	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
496	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~\ref{df:Pfs}
497	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
498	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by $\IPic_{X/S}$.  Moreover, we say simply that the
499	  %  Picard scheme $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists."
500	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch.}
501	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
502	  $k$. The \emph{Picard scheme} $\Pic_{C/k}$ is the $k$-scheme representing
503	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$, supplied by
504	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, equipped with the abelian-group-scheme
505	  structure of \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}. The scheme is separated,
506	  locally of finite type over $k$, and a disjoint union of open
507	  quasi-projective $k$-subschemes.
508	\end{definition}

510	\section{Lean encoding}
511	\label{sec:fga_pic_lean_encoding}
512	
513	The Lean target file is the new
514	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}, sibling to
515	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
516	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) and
517	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
518	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}). The blueprint declarations of
519	this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
520	
521	\begin{itemize}
522	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
523	    (\cref{def:pic_scheme}) is a structural constant naming the scheme
524	    representing $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$; it is the witness extracted
525	    from the \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy} produced by
526	    \texttt{representable} below.
527	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
528	    (\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}) is the morphism of
529	    $k$-schemes $\Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ sending a
530	    relative effective divisor to its associated invertible sheaf.
531	    The construction uses the Hilbert / Quot scheme produced by
532	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (named
533	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme} per the directive's
534	    sibling-chapter naming), restricts to the open subscheme of relative
535	    effective divisors via the Kleiman~\S 3 effective-divisor open
536	    criterion, and composes with the quotient map to
537	    $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
538	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
539	    (\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}) is the Altman--Kleiman
540	    descent of a flat-and-proper equivalence relation $R \subseteq Z
541	    \times_k Z$ on a quasi-projective $k$-scheme $Z$ to a
542	    quasi-projective quotient $Q$, packaged in the form that consumes
543	    a $\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}$-witness for $Z$ and produces
544	    one for the coequalising sheaf.
545	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
546	    (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}) is the assembly: from the
547	    \texttt{abelMap}, the \texttt{QuotScheme} representability witness,
548	    and the \texttt{rel\_pic\_etale\_sheafification} of
549	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, build the
550	    $\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}$-witness for
551	    $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ via the four steps of the proof above
552	    (Hilbert-polynomial stratification, $m$-regularity bound, Abel-map
553	    factorisation, smooth-proper quotient).
554	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
555	    (\cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}) is the
556	    \texttt{CommGroupObject} (or
557	    \texttt{AddCommGrp\_Object}) instance on \texttt{PicScheme} obtained
558	    by Yoneda transport of the abelian-group structure on
559	    $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
560	\end{itemize}
561	
562	No new core axiom is required: this chapter is an assembly chapter on
563	top of the QuotScheme engine, the relative-Picard \'etale sheafification,
564	and the line-bundle pullback machinery already in place. The single
565	large external input (the Altman--Kleiman theorem of effective
566	flat-and-proper equivalence relations on quasi-projective schemes) is
567	recorded as \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient} and is itself a standard
568	result; if its Lean formalisation is not yet in Mathlib, the
569	\texttt{smoothProperQuotient} Lean declaration may be sorried and the

573	\section{Out of scope}
574	\label{sec:fga_pic_out_of_scope}
575	
576	This chapter packages \emph{only} the representable Picard scheme
577	$\Pic_{C/k}$ as an assembly of sibling-chapter inputs, plus the
578	group-scheme refinement. The following downstream constructions live in
579	separate sibling chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
580	\begin{itemize}
581	  \item \textbf{Identity component $\Pic^0_{C/k}$} (A.3) --- the open and
582	    closed subgroup scheme of $\Pic_{C/k}$ containing the unit and
583	    consisting of those classes lying in the connected component of
584	    $\Pic_{C/k}$. Its dimension, smoothness, and abelian-variety
585	    structure are the content of the A.3 chapter
586	    \texttt{Picard\_Pic0AbelianVariety.tex} (planned), gated on this
587	    chapter.
588	  \item \textbf{Quot scheme representability} --- consumed by
589	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} via \texttt{thm:quot\_representable}
