1207	[The $\mathbb G_m$-scaling action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1$ (primary), and the $\mathbb G_a$-translation companion $\sigma$]
1208	  \label{def:gaTranslationP1}
1209	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmScalingP1}
1210	  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects}
1211	  % NOTE (iter-171 refresh of iter-170 decision): the option (c) inline chart-glue
1212	  % commitment LANDED its body skeleton iter-171. `gmScalingP1` now has a CONCRETE
1213	  % body via `Over.homMk ((gmScalingP1_cover).glueMorphisms gmScalingP1_chart
1214	  % gmScalingP1_chart_agreement) gmScalingP1_over_coherence` — no buried sorries.
1215	  % Step A (chart-side ring maps `gmScalingP1_chart{0,1}_ringMap`) + the cover
1216	  % `gmScalingP1_cover` are axiom-clean iter-171. The body's `sorryAx` taint flows
1217	  % through three named top-level internal scaffold sorries:
1218	  % `gmScalingP1_chart` (per-chart scheme morphism), `gmScalingP1_chart_agreement`
1219	  % (cocycle), `gmScalingP1_over_coherence` (over-side coherence). iter-172+ closes
1220	  % these via `pullbackSpecIso ≫ Spec.map _ ≫ Proj.awayι` + `Scheme.Cover.hom_ext`.
1221	  % See `analogies/gmscaling-deep.md` for the full 6-step decomposition.
1222	  % Archon-original Lean encoding; the chart computations below are elementary and characteristic-free.
1223	  % No external % SOURCE QUOTE for the encoding. The mathematical fact -- $\mathbb G_m$ acts on
1224	  % $\mathbb P^1$ by scalings fixing $0$ and $\infty$, $\mathbb G_a$ by translations fixing $\infty$
1225	  % -- is standard (the Borel subgroup of $\mathrm{PGL}_2$ acting on $\mathbb P^1$).
1226	  % NOTE (iter-164/167): the load-bearing action for the PRIMARY genus-0 route is now the
1227	  % MULTIPLICATIVE scaling action $\sigma_\times$ (\lean name gmScalingP1, landed iter-165/166;
1228	  % pinned in this block's \lean{...} hook). The $\mathbb G_a$-translation $\sigma$ (\lean name
1229	  % gaTranslationP1 [expected]) is retained only for the demoted additive alternative and is
1230	  % intentionally not yet a Lean target.
1231	  \textbf{The scaling action (primary).} The \emph{multiplicative scaling action}
1232	  \[ \sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
1233	     \text{``}\sigma_\times(x, \lambda) = \lambda x\text{''} \]
1234	  (\lean name \texttt{gmScalingP1}) is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of
1235	  $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m$ --- no indeterminacy, characteristic-free (M\"obius scaling
1236	  fixing both $0$ and $\infty$) --- defined chartwise:
1237	  \begin{itemize}
1238	    \item On $\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_m$ (target chart $x$): $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$,
1239	      a polynomial map, regular everywhere.
1240	    \item Near $\infty$ (target chart $u = 1/x$): the target coordinate $1/(\lambda x)$ rewrites as
1241	      $u/\lambda$, regular everywhere on this chart because $\lambda \in \mathbb G_m$ is invertible.
1242	  \end{itemize}
1243	  The two target charts cover the image and overlap consistently, so $\sigma_\times$ is defined on
1244	  the whole surface. It is a left action of the group $\mathbb G_m$ on $\mathbb P^1$:
1245	  $\sigma_\times(x, 1) = x$ and $\sigma_\times(\sigma_\times(x, \lambda), \mu) = \sigma_\times(x,
1246	  \lambda\mu)$. Crucially it has \emph{two fixed points}, $\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ and
1247	  $\sigma_\times(\infty, \lambda) = \infty$ for all $\lambda$; the fixed point $0$ is what makes the
1248	  $W$-axis of the additive decomposition collapse in the primary base-case proof
1249	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}).
1250	
1251	  \medskip
1252	  \noindent\textbf{Companion: the $\mathbb G_a$-translation action (demoted route only).} For the
1253	  Milne-faithful additive alternative we also record the \emph{translation action}
1254	  \[ \sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
1255	     \text{``}\sigma(x, y) = x + y\text{''} \]
1256	  (\lean name \texttt{gaTranslationP1} [expected]), likewise a \emph{total} scheme morphism on all
1257	  of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a$, defined chartwise: on $\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_a$
1258	  (chart $x$) it is the polynomial map $(x, y) \mapsto x + y$; near $\infty$ ($u = 1/x$) the
1259	  coordinate $1/(x + y)$ rewrites as $u/(1 + u y)$, regular at $u = 0$ (value $0$ there --- $\sigma$
1260	  fixes $\infty$ for every $y$), while the locus $x + y = 0$ is covered by the chart $x$ on which it
1261	  is the finite value $0$. It is a left action of $\mathbb G_a$ on $\mathbb P^1$ fixing $\infty$:
1262	  $\sigma(x, 0) = x$ and $\sigma(\sigma(x, y), y') = \sigma(x, y + y')$. This companion underlies
1263	  only the alternative proof of \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} and the demoted $\mathbb
1264	  G_a$-additive close of the base case (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}); it is \emph{not} on the
1265	  genus-$0$ critical path.
1266	\end{definition}

1279	\begin{definition}
1280	[Pullback open cover of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m$ along the chart cover of $\mathbb P^1$]
1281	  \label{def:gmscaling_cover}
1282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmScalingP1_cover}
1283	  \uses{def:projlinebar_affine_cover, def:gm}
1284	  % Archon-original Lean encoding: the affine open cover of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m$
1285	  % whose charts the chartwise body of $\sigma_\times$ is built on. No external % SOURCE QUOTE.
1286	  The two-chart affine open cover of $(\mathbb P^1 \otimes \mathbb G_m).\mathrm{left}$ obtained by
1287	  pulling back the chart cover of \cref{def:projlinebar_affine_cover} along the first pullback
1288	  projection $\mathrm{pr}_1 \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1$ (Mathlib's
1289	  $\mathtt{pullback.fst}\,\mathbb P^1.\mathrm{hom}\,\mathbb G_m.\mathrm{hom}$). Concretely the $i$-th
1290	  chart is
1291	  \[ (\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_i =
1292	     \mathrm{pullback}\bigl(\mathtt{pullback.fst}\,\mathbb P^1.\mathrm{hom}\,\mathbb G_m.\mathrm{hom},\
1293	                            \mathrm{Proj.away}\,\iota\,\mathcal A\,X_i\bigr), \]
1294	  i.e.\ the affine open $D_+(X_i) \times \mathbb G_m \subseteq \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m$. This
1295	  cover is the $\mathtt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}$ source on which the body of $\sigma_\times =
1296	  \mathtt{gmScalingP1}$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}) is constructed: each per-chart scheme
1297	  morphism (\cref{def:gmscaling_chart}) is built on chart $(\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_i$, the
1298	  cocycle (\cref{lem:gmscaling_chart_agreement}) checks agreement on each
1299	  pairwise pullback of cover charts, and the over-side coherence
1300	  (\cref{lem:gmscaling_over_coherence}) intertwines the glued total morphism with the structure
1301	  maps to $\Spec \bar k$.
1302	\end{definition}

1304	\begin{definition}
1305	[The chart-$i$ scheme morphism for $\sigma_\times$]
1306	  \label{def:gmscaling_chart}
1307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmScalingP1_chart}
1308	  \uses{def:gmscaling_cover, def:proj_chart_ring_iso, def:projlinebar_affine_cover, def:gm}
1309	  % Archon-original Lean scaffold for the iter-171 chart-glue body of $\sigma_\times$
1310	  % (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). No external % SOURCE QUOTE.
1311	  For each chart index $i \in \{0, 1\} = \mathrm{Fin}\,2$, the chart-$i$ scheme morphism
1312	  \[ \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,i \colon
1313	     (\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover}\,\bar k).X\,i
1314	     \longrightarrow \mathtt{ProjectiveLineBarScheme}\,\bar k \]
1315	  realising $\sigma_\times = \mathtt{gmScalingP1}$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}) on the $i$-th
1316	  chart of the pullback cover $\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover}$ (\cref{def:gmscaling_cover}). On
1317	  chart $1$ (target affine chart $D_+(X_1)$, coordinate $u = X_0/X_1$) the morphism realises
1318	  $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ as the ring-level map $u \mapsto u \otimes \lambda$ in
1319	  $\mathrm{Localization.Away}\,t \otimes_{\bar k} \mathtt{GmRing}\,\bar k$ (here $t$ is the
1320	  $\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k$ generator); on chart $0$ (target affine chart
1321	  $D_+(X_0)$, coordinate $t = X_1/X_0$) it realises $1/(\lambda x) = u/\lambda$ as the ring-level
1322	  map $t \mapsto t \otimes \lambda^{-1}$. The two chart-side ring maps are packaged in Lean as
1323	  $\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart0\_ringMap}$ and $\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart1\_ringMap}$ (both
1324	  axiom-clean iter-171).
1325	
1326	  \medskip
1327	  \noindent\textbf{Construction recipe.} The scheme morphism is assembled from the chart-side
1328	  ring map by the standard ``ring map $\Rightarrow$ scheme map between $\Spec$'s of relative tensor
1329	  products'' bridge, applied to the affine carrier of each cover chart. Concretely the chain is
1330	  $\mathtt{pullbackSpecIso}$ (identifying $(\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_i$ with $\Spec$ of an
1331	  $\bar k$-tensor product of the chart-$i$ chart ring with $\mathtt{GmRing}$), followed by
1332	  $\Spec.\mathrm{map}$ of the ring map above, followed by the chart-ring iso of
1333	  \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} (which identifies $\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$ with the
1334	  affine chart ring $\bar k[u]$ at the $\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k$-level), and then
1335	  $\mathrm{Proj.away}\,\iota$ to land in $\mathbb P^1.\mathrm{left}$. The substantive step that is
1336	  \emph{not} algebraic (the chart-ring iso \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} is already axiom-clean
1337	  via the iter-172 \cref{lem:mvPoly_to_homogeneousLocalization_away_surjective}) is the
1338	  source-side $\mathtt{pullbackSpecIso}$ identification, which navigates the abstract
1339	  $\mathtt{pullback}\,(\mathtt{pullback.fst}\,\mathbb P^1.\mathrm{hom}\,\mathbb G_m.\mathrm{hom})\,
1340	  (\mathrm{Proj.away}\,\iota\,\mathcal A\,X_i)$ into the $\Spec$-of-tensor-product form. The full
1341	  recipe is recorded in the iter-173 analogist note $\mathtt{analogies/chart{\text-}bridge.md}$
1342	  (in flight), and the prior 6-step decomposition in $\mathtt{analogies/gmscaling{\text-}deep.md}$.
1343	\end{definition}

1345	\begin{lemma}
1346	[Cocycle agreement for $\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}$ on pairwise overlaps]
1347	  \label{lem:gmscaling_chart_agreement}
1348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmScalingP1_chart_agreement}
1349	  \uses{def:gmscaling_chart, def:gmscaling_cover}
1350	  % Archon-original Lean scaffold for the iter-171 chart-glue body of $\sigma_\times$
1351	  % (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}). No external % SOURCE QUOTE.
1352	  For every pair of chart indices $x, y \in \mathrm{Fin}\,2$ the two restrictions of
1353	  $\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k$ (\cref{def:gmscaling_chart}) to the pairwise pullback
1354	  $\mathrm{pullback}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_x.f)\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_y.f)$ agree:
1355	  \[ \mathrm{pullback.fst}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_x.f)\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_y.f)
1356	     \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,x
1357	     \ =\
1358	     \mathrm{pullback.snd}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_x.f)\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_y.f)
1359	     \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,y. \]
1360	  This is the cocycle / overlap-coherence equation that $\mathtt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}$
1361	  consumes in order to assemble the chart-wise pieces $\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,i$ into
1362	  a single morphism on the total $(\mathbb P^1 \otimes \mathbb G_m).\mathrm{left}$.
1363	
1364	  \medskip
1365	  \noindent\textbf{Content of the equation.} The diagonal cases $(x, y) = (0, 0)$ and $(x, y) = (1, 1)$
1366	  are trivial via $\mathtt{pullback.condition}$. The substantive cases are the cross cases $(0, 1)$
1367	  and $(1, 0)$: on the basic-open intersection $D_+(X_0 \cdot X_1) \subseteq \mathbb P^1$ both chart
1368	  coordinates are units (the chart-$0$ coordinate $t = X_1/X_0$ and the chart-$1$ coordinate
1369	  $u = X_0/X_1$ are reciprocals: $t \cdot u = 1$), and the agreement reduces to the ring-level
1370	  identity
1371	  \[ \lambda \cdot u \ =\ \frac{1}{t} \cdot \lambda \quad
1372	     \text{in } \mathrm{Localization.Away}\,t \otimes_{\bar k} \mathtt{GmRing}\,\bar k, \]
1373	  i.e.\ to the unit-of-the-localisation identity $t \cdot u = 1$ together with commutativity. The
1374	  proof routes through the chart-ring iso \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso} and the same
1375	  $\mathtt{pullbackSpecIso}$ bridge that \cref{def:gmscaling_chart} uses; consequently
1376	  \cref{lem:gmscaling_chart_agreement} is gated on \cref{def:gmscaling_chart} acquiring a concrete
1377	  body. See $\mathtt{analogies/chart{\text-}bridge.md}$ (iter-173 in flight) for the chart-wise
1378	  recipe.
1379	\end{lemma}

1419	\begin{lemma}
1420	[The $\mathbb G_m$-scaling action fixes the origin $0 \in \mathbb P^1$]
1421	  \label{lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero}
1422	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero}
1423	  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects, def:gaTranslationP1, def:p1bar_zero, def:gm_one}
1424	  % NOTE (iter-171 refresh of iter-170): the Lean body of `gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero`
1425	  % remains a gated `sorry` — but the gate is now NARROWER: iter-171 landed the
1426	  % `gmScalingP1` body SKELETON (concrete `Over.homMk + Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms`),
1427	  % so the residual gate is on the three internal scaffold helpers `gmScalingP1_chart{,
1428	  % _agreement, _over_coherence}`. Once `gmScalingP1_chart` (chart-`i` scheme morphism)
1429	  % has a concrete body, this collapse lemma reduces via chart-1 to the ring-level
1430	  % identity `X 0 ↦ 0 ⊗ λ = 0` in `Localization.Away t ⊗ GmRing` directly.
1431	  % Archon-original; the fact is elementary (σ_×(0, λ) = λ · 0 = 0). No external % SOURCE QUOTE
1432	  % needed for the Lean encoding.
1433	  Let $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1$ be the scaling action
1434	  of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Then the composite
1435	  \[ \mathbb G_m \xrightarrow{\;(\mathrm{toUnit}_{\mathbb G_m} \fatsemi 0_{\mathbb P^1},\ \mathbf 1_{\mathbb G_m})\;}
1436	     \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \xrightarrow{\;\sigma_\times\;} \mathbb P^1 \]
1437	  equals the constant morphism $\mathrm{toUnit}_{\mathbb G_m} \fatsemi 0_{\mathbb P^1}$. That is,
1438	  $\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ for every $\lambda \in \mathbb G_m$.

1441	\begin{proof}
1442	  \uses{def:gaTranslationP1, def:p1bar_zero, def:gm_one}
1443	  Chart-level. On the affine chart $\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_m$ (coordinate $x$), the
1444	  morphism $\sigma_\times$ is the polynomial map $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda \cdot x$. Restricting
1445	  to the $\mathbb G_m$-section $x = 0$ gives the constant ring map
1446	  $\bar k[t, t^{-1}] \to \bar k$, $\lambda \mapsto 0$; equivalently the scheme morphism factors
1447	  through the closed point $0 \in \mathbb P^1$. The agreement with the other chart $u = 1/x$ near
1448	  $\infty$ is automatic (the section $x = 0$ does not meet that chart). The named equation is the
1449	  Lean encoding of this fact, with $\mathrm{toUnit}_{\mathbb G_m}$ supplying the
1450	  $\Spec \bar k$-factor and $\mathbf 1_{\mathbb G_m}$ the parameter $\lambda$.
1451	\end{proof}

1453	\begin{lemma}
1454	[$\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$: a homomorphism from the additive group into an abelian variety is trivial]
1455	  \label{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1456	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1457	  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:hom_additivity_over_product, lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
1458	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 25--26)
1459	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.9. Every rational map A¹ ⇢ A or P¹ ⇢ A is constant."
1460	  % NOTE (iter-164): the verbatim Milne Prop 3.9 statement+proof quotes are the prior-session-verified
1461	  % copies also carried on \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} below. This host lacks a PDF renderer
1462	  % (pdftoppm) so the quotes were NOT re-rendered from references/abelian-varieties.pdf this session;
1463	  % they are reproduced from the verified in-tree copy. See the writer report.
1464	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1465	
1466	  \textbf{Off the genus-$0$ critical path (iter-164).} This $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
1467	  lemma is \emph{superseded by the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut}
1468	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}), which closes the
1469	  base case without it. It is retained only as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9 ($\mathbb
1470	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility) argument feeding the alternative route
1471	  \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}; no block on the genus-$0$ critical path depends on it.
1472	
1473	  Let $A$ be an abelian variety over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$, and let
1474	  $\varphi \colon \mathbb G_a \to A$ be a homomorphism of group objects (so $\varphi(0) = 0_A$ and
1475	  $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$). Then $\varphi$ is the trivial homomorphism: it
1476	  factors through the terminal object as the constant morphism at the identity,
1477	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1478	
1479	  \medskip
1480	  \noindent\textbf{Equivalent completeness phrasing.} Equivalently: a homomorphism from an
1481	  \emph{affine connected} group object into a \emph{complete} group object is trivial. That is the
1482	  abstract content; the proof below realises it characteristic-free.
1483	\end{lemma}
1484	
1485	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1486	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1487	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1488	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1489	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1490	\begin{proof}
1491	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
1492	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1493	  Write $A$ additively (an abelian variety is commutative). We give the conceptual
1494	  \emph{completeness} proof as the abstract argument, then record Milne's elementary $\mathbb
1495	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility, which applies whenever $\varphi$ extends to a morphism on
1496	  $\mathbb P^1$ (as it does in the genus-$0$ application).
1497	
1498	  \textbf{Completeness proof.} The image $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is a connected subgroup of $A$
1499	  (image of the connected $\mathbb G_a$ under a homomorphism). As the image of a homomorphism of
1500	  algebraic groups it is a \emph{closed} subgroup of $A$, hence \emph{complete} (closed in the
1501	  complete $A$); as a homomorphic image of the \emph{affine} $\mathbb G_a$ it is moreover
1502	  \emph{affine}. A connected variety that is simultaneously complete and affine over $\bar k$ is a
1503	  single point: its ring of global regular functions is a field finite over $\bar k$, hence equal
1504	  to $\bar k$ (the $\Gamma = \bar k$ collapse already packaged for proper integral sources in
1505	  \cref{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}). Therefore $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is one point;
1506	  being a homomorphism, $\varphi$ sends $0$ there, so the point is $0_A$ and
1507	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1508	
1509	  \medskip
1510	  \noindent\textbf{Milne's elementary $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility (when $\varphi$
1511	  extends to $\mathbb P^1$).} Suppose $\varphi = f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ for a morphism
1512	  $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ (the genus-$0$ situation). Set $g := f - f(1)$, so $g(1) = 0_A$, and
1513	  form $h_\times := \sigma_\times \fatsemi g \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A$ with the
1514	  scaling companion $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Applying the additive
1515	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (complete),
1516	  $W = \mathbb G_m$, base points $1 \in \mathbb P^1$ and $1 \in \mathbb G_m$ (so
1517	  $h_\times(1, 1) = g(1) = 0_A$), the axis restrictions are $x \mapsto g(x)$ and
1518	  $\lambda \mapsto g(\lambda)$, whence $g(\lambda x) = g(x) + g(\lambda)$ on $\bar k^\times$;
1519	  re-expanding $g$ gives the multiplicative relation
1520	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$ with $c = -\varphi(1)$. Now $\varphi$ is at once
1521	  additive, $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$, and ``multiplicative-additive'',
1522	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$. Computing $\varphi((x + x')\,y)$ in two ways for
1523	  $x, x' \in \bar k$, $y \in \bar k^\times$,
1524	  \[ \varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(xy) + \varphi(x'y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') + 2\varphi(y) + 2c, \]
1525	  while also $\varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(x+x') + \varphi(y) + c = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') +
1526	  \varphi(y) + c$; equating gives $\varphi(y) = -c = \varphi(1)$ for every $y \in \bar k^\times$.
1527	  Hence $\varphi$ is constant on the dense $\mathbb G_m \subset \mathbb G_a$, so constant on
1528	  $\mathbb G_a$ (density, $A$ separated), with value $\varphi(0) = 0_A$. This is Milne's argument
1529	  verbatim and uses only the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} plus the scaling action,
1530	  avoiding the ``image is a closed subgroup'' input of the completeness proof. \qedhere
1531	\end{proof}
1532	
1533	\begin{remark}
1534	  \label{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}
1535	  \textbf{The primary route is the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut; why no Theorem~3.2 and no
1536	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ are needed.} The genus-$0$ base case ``$f \colon \mathbb P^1
1537	  \to A$ is constant'' is closed \emph{directly} by the proven additive decomposition
1538	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} composed with a total $\mathbb G$-action on $\mathbb P^1$,
1539	  with \emph{no} appeal to Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension), the theorem of
1540	  the cube, Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials. The reason the earlier draft thought
1541	  Theorem~3.2 was needed --- that the additive defect $\psi(x,y) = f(x+y) - f(x) - f(y)$ lives only
1542	  on the affine $\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a$ and must be extended over $\mathbb P^1 \times
1543	  \mathbb P^1$ --- evaporates once one composes with a \emph{total} action morphism of
1544	  \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}: the composite is already a total morphism out of $\mathbb P^1 \times
1545	  \mathbb G$, and the complete factor is $\mathbb P^1$, exactly what
1546	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (whose Lean signature needs only the first factor
1547	  complete) consumes.
1548	
1549	  \medskip
1550	  \noindent\textbf{Primary route --- the scaling shortcut (strategy decision of iter-164).} The
1551	  \emph{multiplicative} scaling action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
1552	  \mathbb P^1$, $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}), closes the base case
1553	  in one shot, \emph{without} the additive homomorphism step or \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} at
1554	  all. Normalise $f(0) = 0_A$ (translate; here $0$ is a scaling fixed point). Form $h_\times :=
1555	  \sigma_\times \fatsemi f$ and apply \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with $V = \mathbb P^1$,
1556	  $W = \mathbb G_m$, base points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$ and $1 \in \mathbb G_m$ (so $h_\times(0, 1) =
1557	  f(0) = 0_A$). Because $0$ is a \emph{fixed point} of scaling, the $W$-axis restriction collapses:
1558	  $\lambda \mapsto h_\times(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$. The decomposition
1559	  therefore reads $h_\times = p \fatsemi f$ with the second summand the group identity, i.e.
1560	  \[ \sigma_\times \fatsemi f = \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A,
1561	     \qquad\text{``}f(\lambda x) = f(x)\text{''}. \]
1562	  Precomposing with $\lambda \mapsto (1, \lambda)$ gives $f|_{\mathbb G_m} = \text{const}\,f(1)$;
1563	  since $\mathbb G_m$ is dense in $\mathbb P^1$ and $A$ is separated, $f$ is constant on $\mathbb
1564	  P^1$ (the dominant-source/separated-target handle \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}, i.e.\ the
1565	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ family already used by \texttt{rigidity\_core}). This uses
1566	  \emph{only} the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, the total morphism $\sigma_\times$,
1567	  and density --- no $\mathbb G_a$ additivity, no \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, and in
1568	  particular \emph{no} ``image of a group homomorphism is a closed subgroup'' input (a deep Mathlib
1569	  gap). It is the least-Mathlib-blocked realisation of the base case and is the route the live
1570	  consumer \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} formalises.
1571	
1572	  \medskip
1573	  \noindent\textbf{Retained alternative --- the $\mathbb G_a$-additive route.} The Milne-faithful
1574	  argument routes through the translation action $\sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to
1575	  \mathbb P^1$: $\sigma \fatsemi f$ is total, the additive decomposition makes $f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ a
1576	  homomorphism $\mathbb G_a \to A$ (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}), and a homomorphism from the
1577	  additive group into an abelian variety is trivial (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, Milne's
1578	  $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility, Proposition~3.9). This reaches the same conclusion but
1579	  needs the triviality lemma; it is retained for fidelity to Milne and is \emph{off} the genus-$0$
1580	  critical path.
1581	\end{remark}

1583	\begin{lemma}
1584	[A pointed $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (Milne Prop 3.9, via the 4th route)]
1585	  \label{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}
1586	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_Ga_to_av_const}
1587	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1588	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 25--26)
1589	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.9. Every rational map A¹ ⇢ A or P¹ ⇢ A is constant."
1590	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1591	
1592	  \textbf{Off the genus-$0$ critical path (iter-164): retained alternative route.} This
1593	  $\mathbb G_a$-additive close of the base case is \emph{superseded by the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
1594	  shortcut} now installed as the proof of \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} (see
1595	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). It is retained as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9
1596	  argument (faithful to the $\mathbb G_a \to A$ homomorphism / $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
1597	  presentation), but the live consumer \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} no longer depends on
1598	  it; it is provided here only for completeness.

1600	  Let $A$ be an abelian variety over $\bar k$ and $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ a
1601	  morphism with $f(0) = 0_A$ (the additive-identity $\bar k$-point of $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1
1602	  \subset \mathbb P^1$; any base point reduces to this by a normalising translation in $A$).
1603	  Then $f$ is constant. Concretely: the translation action $\sigma$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1})
1604	  makes $f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ an additive-group homomorphism $\mathbb G_a \to A$, and a homomorphism
1605	  $\mathbb G_a \to A$ is trivial (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), so $f$ collapses to the
1606	  constant $0_A$.
1607	\end{lemma}
1608	
1609	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1610	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1611	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1612	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1613	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1614	\begin{proof}
1615	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1616	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1617	  Normalise $f(0) = 0_A$ by a translation in $A$ (un-translate at the end). The proof is the
1618	  \emph{4th route} (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}): the additive defect map is never built on
1619	  the affine surface and never extended; instead we precompose $f$ with the \emph{total}
1620	  translation action $\sigma$, and the complete factor is $\mathbb P^1$ itself, so the proven
1621	  additive decomposition applies directly. Milne's Theorem~3.2
1622	  (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is \emph{not} used.
1623	
1624	  \textbf{$f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ is an additive homomorphism, via the total action $\sigma$.} Form
1625	  \[ h := \sigma \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to A, \qquad
1626	     \text{``}h(x, y) = f(x + y)\text{''}, \]
1627	  using the translation action $\sigma$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}; this is a \emph{total}
1628	  morphism (no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
1629	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
1630	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (proper), $W = \mathbb G_a$, and base
1631	  points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $0 \in \mathbb G_a$, noting $h(0, 0) = f(0) = 0_A$. The axis
1632	  restrictions are $x \mapsto h(x, 0) = f(x)$ and $y \mapsto h(0, y) = f(y)$, so the decomposition
1633	  $h = (p \fatsemi f) + (q \fatsemi (f|_{\mathbb G_a}))$ reads, on $\bar k$-points,
1634	  \[ f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y), \qquad x \in \mathbb P^1(\bar k),\ y \in \mathbb G_a(\bar k) = \bar k. \]
1635	  Restricting $x$ to $\mathbb G_a$ shows $f|_{\mathbb G_a} \colon \mathbb G_a \to A$ is a
1636	  homomorphism of group objects (it sends $0 \mapsto 0_A$ and respects addition).
1637	
1638	  \textbf{A homomorphism $\mathbb G_a \to A$ is trivial.} By \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1639	  (the $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ lemma, the content of Milne's $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$
1640	  incompatibility), $f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ is the constant morphism at $0_A$. Hence $f$ is constant on
1641	  the dense open $\mathbb G_a \subset \mathbb P^1$; since $\mathbb G_a$ is dense and $A$ is
1642	  separated, $f$ is constant on all of $\mathbb P^1$, with value $f(0) = 0_A$ (i.e.\ the original
1643	  base value before the normalising translation). \qedhere

1645	  \medskip
1646	  \noindent\emph{Note: this route is the demoted alternative.} The primary base-case proof is the
1647	  $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut now installed directly as the proof of
1648	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}): the multiplicative
1649	  scaling action $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1} gives $\sigma_\times \fatsemi f =
1650	  \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f$ (the $W$-axis collapses because $0$ is a scaling fixed point), so
1651	  $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is constant and $f$ is constant by density --- bypassing both the $\mathbb
1652	  G_a$-additivity step and \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, and avoiding the ``image is a closed
1653	  subgroup'' Mathlib gap. The present $\mathbb G_a$-additive lemma is retained only for fidelity to
1654	  Milne; it is not on the genus-$0$ critical path.
1655	\end{proof}

1658	
1659	\begin{proposition}
1660

1663	  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
1664	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
1665	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}

1667	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
1668	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
1669	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field

1671	  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
1672	  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an

1674	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case $V = \mathbb P^1$).}
1675	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $A$ an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then every
1676	  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a
1677	  single $\bar k$-point.

1690	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We give the project's \textbf{primary} proof, the
1691	  \emph{$\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut}: it proves constancy \emph{in one shot} from the proven
1692	  additive decomposition (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and a scaling fixed point,
1693	  without the intermediate ``$f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ additive'' step and \emph{without} the

1696	  nonsingular $\mathbb P^1$, so Milne's Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is
1697	  \emph{not} invoked either.

1700	  \mathbb P^1$ (one of the two fixed points of $\sigma_\times$). Translating in the group $A$ by
1701	  $-f(0)$, we may assume $f(0) = 0_A$; un-translating at the end restores the original value.

1704	  \[ h := \sigma_\times \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A, \qquad
1705	     \text{``}h(x, \lambda) = f(\lambda x)\text{''}, \]
1706	  using the multiplicative scaling action $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1} (a genuine
1707	  total morphism --- no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
1708	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
1709	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (proper), $W = \mathbb G_m$, and base
1710	  points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $1 \in \mathbb G_m$; here $h(0, 1) = f(1 \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$, as
1711	  required. The decomposition writes $h = (p \fatsemi f_0) + (q \fatsemi g)$ with $f_0$ the
1712	  $V$-axis restriction $x \mapsto h(x, 1) = f(1 \cdot x) = f(x)$ (so $f_0 = f$) and $g$ the
1713	  $W$-axis restriction $\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$.

1787	\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
1788	
1789	\begin{proposition}
1790	
1791	\leanok
1792	[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
1793	  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1794	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1795	  \uses{def:genus}
1796	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
1797	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
1798	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
1799	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now

1800	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
1801	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
1802	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
1803	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
1804	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
1805	  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
1806	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
1807	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
1808	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1809	\end{proposition}
1810	
1811	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
1812	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
1813	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
1814	\begin{proof}
1815	  \uses{def:genus}
1816	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
1817	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
1818	
1819	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
1820	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
1821	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
1822	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
1823	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
1824	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
1825	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
1826	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
1827	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
1828	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
1829	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1830	\end{proof}
1831	
1832	\begin{remark}
1833	  \label{rmk:genusZero_iso_subbuild}

1835	  genuine sub-build, not a short step: Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch theorem for curves, no
1836	  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of
1837	  rational curves. The statement is stated here at textbook level so the chain is complete;
1838	  closing its Lean proof will require either importing/porting a substantial slice of curve
1839	  theory or re-deriving the genus-$0 \Rightarrow \mathbb P^1$ classification from the
1840	  project's own $\genus$ definition. The dependency on $\genus$ (\cref{def:genus}) is the link
1841	  to \cref{chap:Genus}.
1842	\end{remark}

1845	
1846	\begin{theorem}
1847	
1848	\leanok
1849	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
1850	  \label{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
1851	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
1852	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
1853	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1854	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
1855	  $\mathtt{CharZero}$ hypothesis). Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
1856	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
1857	  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
1858	  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
1859	  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
1860	  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1861	
1862	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
1863	  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
1864	  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
1865	  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
1866	  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
1867	  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
1868	  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
1869	  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
1870	  \eta[A]$.
1871	\end{theorem}
1872	
1873	\begin{proof}
1874	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1875	  \leanok
1876	  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
1877	  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
1878	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
1879	  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
1880	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
1881	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
1882	  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
1883	  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
1884	  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
1885	  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1886	
1887	  No differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre-duality decomposition,
1888	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.

1890	  $\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ be dropped, and (after the $\bar k \to k$ descent step hosted
1891	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}$.
1892

314	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on $X$. A prime divisor is just
315	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
316	  % P_i$, where the $P_i$ are closed points, and $n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
317	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
318	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over a field $k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
319	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to $P$} is the element
320	  \[
321	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \Div(C)
322	  \]
323	  i.e.\ the prime divisor $P$ with multiplicity one. The assignment is
324	  well-defined because the closed point $P$ is automatically a codimension-$1$
325	  integral closed subscheme of the $1$-dimensional integral scheme $C$.
326	  Conversely, every prime divisor on $C$ is of this form, so an arbitrary
327	  divisor on $C$ is a finite formal $\mathbb Z$-linear combination
328	  $\sum n_i [P_i]$ of closed points.
329	
330	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
331	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
332	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a hypothesis
333	  \texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}. It does \emph{not} typeclass-pin
334	  ``smooth proper curve over $\bar k$'' at the carrier level; the
335	  well-definedness of \texttt{ofClosedPoint} as a \texttt{PrimeDivisor}
336	  promotion goes through a separate argument that, on a one-dimensional
337	  integral scheme, \texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} implies
338	  \texttt{Order.coheight P = 1}. The prover-agent threads the appropriate
339	  curve-level typeclasses inside the proof body, not in the signature; the
340	  blueprint-level statement is the one in the prose above.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
344	
345	\begin{definition}
346	
347	\leanok
348	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over $\bar k$]
349	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
350	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
351	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_closed_point}
352	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
353	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
354	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
355	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of $D$ to be $\sum n_i$."
356	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
357	  nonsingular curve).}
358	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
359	  $\bar k$. The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
360	  \Div(C)$ is the integer
361	  \[
362	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
363	  \]
364	  The definition uses that every closed point of $C$ has residue field
365	  $\bar k$ (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
366	  field $k$ the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
367	  $\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]$ recovers the
368	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
369	  $\bar k$ specialisation.
370	
371	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
372	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
373	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
374	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
375	  set ``smooth proper curve over $\bar k$'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
376	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
377	  $D \in \Div(X)$ on any scheme $X$ the integer-valued sum of its
378	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ this coincides
379	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
380	  residue field $\bar k$, so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
381	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
382	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
383	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
384	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
385	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
386	  (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
387	\end{definition}
388	
389	\begin{theorem}
390	
391	\leanok
392	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
393	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
395	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
396	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
397	  $\bar k$. The degree map of \Cref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
398	  homomorphism
399	  \[
400	    \deg \colon \Div(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
401	    \qquad
402	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
403	    \qquad
404	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
405	  \]
406	\end{theorem}
407	
408	\begin{proof}
409	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
410	  \leanok
411	  Immediate from the definition: $\Div(C)$ is the free abelian group on the
412	  set of closed points of $C$, and $\deg$ is the unique group homomorphism
413	  sending each generator $[P]$ to $1 \in \mathbb Z$ (equivalently: the sum
414	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
415	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
416	\end{proof}

418	\section{Principal divisors}
419	
420	\begin{definition}
421	
422	\leanok
423	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
424	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
425	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
426	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
427	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of $\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)$,
428	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
429	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$, and let $f \in K^\ast$
430	  % be a nonzero function on $X$. Then $v_Y(f) = 0$ for all except finitely
431	  % many prime divisors $Y$."
432	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$ and let $f \in K^\ast$.
433	  % We define the \emph{divisor of $f$}, denoted $(f)$, by
434	  % $(f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y$, where the sum is taken over all prime
435	  % divisors $Y$ of $X$. By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
436	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
437	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
438	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
439	  p.~131.}
440	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$,
441	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that $\ord_Y(f) = 0$ for all but finitely
442	  many prime divisors $Y$ on $X$; hence the formal sum
443	  \[
444	    \div(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
445	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \Div(X)
446	  \]
447	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
448	  \emph{principal divisor of $f$}. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over $\bar k$
449	  the same definition specialises to
450	  $\div(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]$, the formal
451	  sum over closed points.
452	\end{definition}
453	
454	\begin{theorem}
455	
456	\leanok
457	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
458	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
459	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
460	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
461	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
462	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
463	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
464	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if $f, g \in K^\ast$, then $(f/g) = (f) - (g)$
465	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
466	  % $f$ to its divisor $(f)$ gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
467	  % group $K^\ast$ to the additive group $\operatorname{Div} X$, and the
468	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
469	  % $\operatorname{Div} X$."
470	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
471	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. The principal-divisor map
472	  $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ is a group homomorphism from the
473	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
474	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
475	  \[
476	    \div(fg) \;=\; \div(f) + \div(g),
477	    \qquad
478	    \div(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\div(f),
479	    \qquad
480	    \div(1) \;=\; 0.
481	  \]
482	\end{theorem}
483	
484	\begin{proof}
485	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
486	  \leanok
487	  Each prime divisor $Y$ on $X$ contributes a discrete valuation $v_Y$ on
488	  $K(X)$ (\Cref{def:order_at_point}); by the standard DVR axioms,
489	  $v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)$ and $v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)$ for $f, g \in
490	  K(X)^{\times}$, and $v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
491	  divisors of $X$ (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
492	  \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, so the support of the sum is the union
493	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
494	  identities.
495	\end{proof}
496	
497	\begin{theorem}
498	
499	\leanok

255	
256	\begin{definition}
257	
258	\leanok
259	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
260	  \label{def:order_at_point}
261	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
262	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
263	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$ of a prime
264	  % divisor; "zero/pole of $f$ along $Y$") (read from
265	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
266	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
267	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its
268	  % generic point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at
269	  % $\eta$ is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the
270	  % function field of $X$. We call the corresponding discrete valuation
271	  % $v_Y$ the \emph{valuation of $Y$}."
272	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let $f \in K^\ast$ be any nonzero rational function
273	  % on $X$. Then $v_Y(f)$ is an integer. If it is positive, we say $f$ has
274	  % a \emph{zero} along $Y$, of that order; if it is negative, we say $f$
275	  % has a \emph{pole} along $Y$, of order $-v_Y(f)$."
276	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$).}
277	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a prime divisor $Y \subset X$ with generic
278	  point $\eta \in Y$, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a discrete
279	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
280	  function field $K(X)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$
281	  the \emph{order of $f$ along $Y$} is the integer
282	  \[
283	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
284	  \]
285	  where $v_Y$ is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
286	  $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$. If $\ord_Y(f) > 0$, $f$ has a \emph{zero of
287	  order $\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}; if $\ord_Y(f) < 0$, $f$ has a \emph{pole
288	  of order $-\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}.
289	
290	  Specialisation to a curve. If $C$ is a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$
291	  and $P \in C$ is a closed point, then $P$ is automatically a prime
292	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the $1$-dimensional
293	  scheme $C$); the local ring $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation
294	  ring by smoothness, and $\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)$ is the standard DVR
295	  valuation of $f$ at $P$.
296	\end{definition}

301	
302	\leanok
303	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.a sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem, the technical heart that
8	% turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
9	% morphism $X \to S$ with coherent input $\F$ into a finite collection of
10	% locally-closed strata over which $\F$ becomes flat. Mathlib (master
11	% \texttt{b80f227}) has neither a coherent-sheaf flatness predicate over a
12	% scheme base nor the flattening-stratification construction; this chapter
13	% is the prover-ready specification for the new file
14	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean} which a
15	% subsequent iter (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
16	%
17	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
18	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
19	
20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme $\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of $\Quot$ into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf $\F$ on a projective
30	$S$-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of $S$ on each of which
31	the restriction of $\F$ becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme $S$, a projective morphism
40	$\pi : X \to S$, and a coherent $\OO_X$-module $\F$. The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective $X \to S$, which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where $S = T$ is an arbitrary
43	$k$-scheme, $X = C \times_k T$ for $C$ a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	$k$, and $\F$ is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \S\ref{sec:flatstrat_curve_application} below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
52	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
53	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
54	  Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $f : X \to S$ a morphism of finite type, and
55	  $\F$ a coherent $\OO_X$-module.  We say $\F$ is \emph{flat over $S$}
56	  (equivalently, $\F$ is \emph{$f$-flat}, or \emph{$S$-flat}) if for every
57	  point $x \in X$ the stalk $\F_x$ is a flat $\OO_{S, f(x)}$-module, where the
58	  $\OO_{S, f(x)}$-module structure is given by the composite
59	  $\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)$.
60	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
61	  passim.}
62	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open $U = \Spec A \subseteq S$ and
63	  an affine open $V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)$ with $\F|_V = \widetilde{M}$
64	  for $M$ a finite $B$-module), $\F|_V$ is $\OO_U$-flat in the sense that $M$ is a
65	  flat $A$-module (with the $A$-module structure coming from the ring map
66	  $A \to B$).
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\begin{remark}
70	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
71	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism $g : S' \to S$,
72	  pulling back along the cartesian square
73	  \[
74	    \begin{tikzcd}
75	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
76	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
77	    \end{tikzcd}
78	  \]
79	  yields a coherent $\OO_{X'}$-module $\F' := (g')^* \F$ which is $S'$-flat
80	  whenever $\F$ is $S$-flat.  This is the routine cancellation of tensor-product
81	  associativity at the level of stalks. The converse fails in general.
82	\end{remark}
83	
84	\section{Generic flatness}
85	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
86	
87	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
88	induction on $S$. The induction step relies on the following classical
89	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
90	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
91	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
92	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
93	non-zero element of the base ring.
94	
95	\begin{theorem}
96	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
97	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
98	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
99	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
100	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
102	  % "Let $A$ be a noetherian domain,
103	  %  and $B$ a finite type $A$ algebra. Let $M$ be a finite $B$-module.
104	  %  Then there exists an $f\in A$, $f\ne 0$, such that
105	  %  the localisation $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$."
106	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
107	  Let $A$ be a noetherian domain, $B$ a finite-type $A$-algebra, and $M$ a finite
108	  $B$-module. Then there exists $f \in A$, $f \ne 0$, such that the localisation
109	  $M_f$ is a free $A_f$-module.
110	\end{theorem}
111	
112	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field $K$ of $A$, the $K$-algebra
113	% $B_K = K\otimes _AB$ is of finite type, and $M_K = K\otimes_AM$ is a
114	% finite module over $B_K$. Let $n$ be the dimension of the support
115	% of $M_K$ over $\spec B_K$. We argue by induction on $n$, starting
116	% with $n=-1$ which is the case when $M_K =0$. [...]
117	% Now let $n\ge 0$, and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
118	% As $B$ is noetherian and $M$ is assumed to be a finite $B$-module,
119	% there exists a finite filtration
120	% $0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M$
121	% where each $M_i$ is a $B$-submodule of $M$ such that for each $i\ge 1$
122	% the quotient module $M_i/M_{i-1}$ is isomorphic to $B/\pp_i$ for
123	% some prime ideal $\pp_i$ in $B$. [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
124	% there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$, such that $K\otimes_AB$ is
125	% finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ [...]"
126	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
127	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
130	  Following [Nitsure], \S4: pass to the fraction field $K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)$ and
131	  set $B_K = K \otimes_A B$, $M_K = K \otimes_A M$, $n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K$.
132	  Argue by induction on $n$, with base case $n = -1$ (i.e.\ $M_K = 0$): each
133	  generator of $M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$; taking the
134	  product of finitely many annihilators yields $f \in A$ with $f M = 0$, hence
135	  $M_f = 0$ is trivially free.  For $n \ge 0$, a prime filtration of $M$ as a
136	  finite $B$-module
137	  \[
138	    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
139	  \]
140	  with successive quotients $M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i$ reduces the
141	  problem (via the splicing fact: $M_{f'f''}$ is $A_{f'f''}$-free when $M'_{f'}$
142	  and $M''_{f''}$ are free) to the case $M = B/\mathfrak{p}$ for a prime
143	  $\mathfrak{p} \subset B$, then to the case $B$ a domain and $M = B$.
144	  Noether normalisation applied to $B_K / K$ yields algebraically independent
145	  $b_1, \dots, b_n \in B$ with $B_K$ finite over $K[b_1, \dots, b_n]$; choosing
146	  $g \ne 0$ to clear denominators of integrality equations, $B_g$ is finite over
147	  $A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]$. Let $m$ be the generic rank: the short exact sequence
148	  \[
149	    0 \to A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0
150	  \]
151	  has $T$ a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]$, so $\dim
152	  \mathrm{supp}_{B_K} (K \otimes_{A_g} T) < n$.  The inductive hypothesis
153	  yields $h \ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$; set $f := gh$.
154	\end{proof}
155	
156	\begin{theorem}
157	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
158	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
160	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat, thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
161	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
162	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
163	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
164	  % "Let $S$ be a noetherian and integral scheme.
165	  %  Let $p: X\to S$ be a finite type morphism, and let $\F$ be a
166	  %  coherent sheaf of ${\mathcal O}_X$-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
167	  %  open subscheme $U\subset S$ such that the restriction of $\F$
168	  %  to $X_U = p^{-1}(U)$ is flat over ${\mathcal O}_U$."
169	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
170	  Let $S$ be a noetherian integral scheme, $p : X \to S$ a finite-type morphism,
171	  and $\F$ a coherent $\OO_X$-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
172	  subscheme $U \subseteq S$ such that the restriction of $\F$ to
173	  $X_U = p^{-1}(U)$ is $\OO_U$-flat.
174	\end{theorem}
175	
176	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
177	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of $S$."
178	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
179	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
180	% Theorem~\ref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} by restricting to a non-empty
181	% affine open of $S$, where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
182	\begin{proof}
183	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
184	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open $\Spec A \subseteq S$ where $A$ is a
185	  noetherian domain. The morphism $p$ is of finite type, so locally on $X$
186	  above this affine $\F$ is the sheaf $\widetilde{M}$ for $M$ a finite module
187	  over a finite-type $A$-algebra $B$. Theorem~\ref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
188	  produces $f \in A$, $f \ne 0$, with $M_f$ free over $A_f$ on each such patch;
189	  shrinking to the basic open $D(f) \subseteq \Spec A$ globalises by patching
190	  finitely many such opens (using noetherianity of $X$ above the affine).
191	\end{proof}
