193	\section{Existence of the flattening stratification}
194	\label{sec:flatstrat_exists}
195	
196	\begin{theorem}
197	[Existence of the flattening stratification]
198	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
199	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification}
200	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat, thm:generic_flatness}

200	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat, thm:generic_flatness}
201	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification''
202	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1811-L1841).
203	  % Parallel pointer: [Stacks Project], tag 052H (verbatim text not yet
204	  % retrieved into a local references/ file; see ``Notes for Plan Agent'').
205	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
206	  % "Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, and let
207	  %  $\F$ be a coherent sheaf on the projective space $\PP^n_S$
208	  %  over $S$. Then the set $I$ of Hilbert polynomials of
209	  %  restrictions of $\F$ to fibers of $\PP^n_S\to S$
210	  %  is a finite set. Moreover, for each $f\in I$ there exist
211	  %  a locally closed subscheme $S_f$ of $S$,
212	  %  such that the following conditions are satisfied.
213	  %
214	  %  \textbf{(i) Point-set: } The underlying set $|S_f|$ of $S_f$ consists of
215	  %  all points $s\in S$ where the Hilbert polynomial of the
216	  %  restriction of $\F$ to $\PP^n_s$ is $f$. In particular,
217	  %  the subsets $|S_f|\subset |S|$ are disjoint,
218	  %  and their set-theoretic union is $|S|$.
219	  %
220	  %  \textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
221	  %  Let $S'=\coprod S_f$ be the coproduct of the $S_f$, and let
222	  %  $i:S'\to S$ be the morphism induced by the inclusions
223	  %  $S_f\hra S$. Then the sheaf $i^*(\F)$ on $\PP^n_{S'}$
224	  %  is flat over $S'$. Moreover, $i:S'\to S$ has the universal property that
225	  %  for any morphism $\varphi :T\to S$ the pullback $\varphi^*(\F)$ on
226	  %  $\PP^n_T$ is flat over $T$ if and only if $\varphi$ factors through
227	  %  $i:S'\to S$.  The subscheme $S_f$ is
228	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial $f$.
229	  %
230	  %  \textbf{(iii) Closure of strata: }
231	  %  Let the set $I$ of Hilbert polynomials be given a total
232	  %  ordering, defined by putting $f<g$ whenever $f(n) < g(n)$ for all
233	  %  $n \gg  0$. Then the closure in $S$ of the subset $|S_f|$
234	  %  is contained in the union of all $|S_g|$ where $f\le g$."
235	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'';
236	  cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 052H.}
237	  Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme and $\F$ a coherent sheaf on the relative
238	  projective space $\PP^n_S$ over $S$.  Then:
239	  \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
240	    \item The set $I$ of Hilbert polynomials
241	      $P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))$ that arise as $s$ ranges over $S$ is
242	      finite.
243	    \item For each $f \in I$ there exists a locally-closed subscheme
244	      $S_f \hookrightarrow S$ whose underlying set $|S_f|$ consists of exactly
245	      those $s \in S$ with $P_s = f$.  The collection $\{|S_f|\}_{f \in I}$ is
246	      pairwise disjoint with set-theoretic union $|S|$.  Forming the coproduct
247	      $S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f$ and the morphism $i : S' \to S$ assembled
248	      from the inclusions $S_f \hookrightarrow S$, the pullback $i^* \F$ on
249	      $\PP^n_{S'}$ is flat over $S'$, and $i : S' \to S$ is universal with this
250	      property: for any morphism $\varphi : T \to S$ such that $\varphi^* \F$ on
251	      $\PP^n_T$ is flat over $T$, $\varphi$ factors uniquely through $i$.
252	      Each $S_f$ is uniquely determined by $f$.
253	    \item Order $I$ by $f < g \iff f(n) < g(n)$ for all $n \gg 0$. Then the
254	      closure of $|S_f|$ in $|S|$ is contained in $\bigcup_{g \ge f} |S_g|$.
255	  \end{enumerate}
256	\end{theorem}
257	
258	The proof is long; we split it into sub-lemmas below.  The first sub-lemma is
259	the special case $n = 0$ (when $\PP^n_S = S$ and $\F$ is just a coherent sheaf
260	on $S$), which already contains the flat-locus-as-locally-closed-subscheme
261	mechanism. The remaining inputs are (A) the Noetherian-induction reduction to
262	the flat case via generic flatness; (B) uniform vanishing of higher cohomology
263	of $\F(m)$ for $m \gg 0$ on $\F_{V_i}$ for the flat pieces; and (C) base-change
264	isomorphisms for the direct images $\pi_* \F(m)$, which allow comparing
265	strata for different twists.
266	
267	\begin{lemma}
268	[Flat locus on $S$ for a coherent sheaf is a locally-closed stratification]
269	  \label{lem:flat_locus_open}
270	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
271	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Special case
272	  % $n=0$ (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
273	  % L1849-L1885).
274	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
275	  % "\textbf{Special case: } Let $n=0$, so that $\PP^n_S=S$.
276	  %  For any $s\in S$, the \textbf{fiber} $\F|_s$ of $\F$ over $s$
277	  %  will mean the pull-back of $\F$ to the subscheme
278	  %  $\spec \kappa(s)$ [...]. The Hilbert polynomial of the
279	  %  restriction of $\F$ to the fiber over $s$ is the degree $0$
280	  %  polynomial $e \in \Q[\lambda]$, where $e = \dim_{\kappa(s)}\F|_s$.
281	  %
282	  %  By Nakayama lemma, any basis of $\F|_s$ prolongs to a neighbourhood $U$ of $s$
283	  %  to give a set of generators for $F|_U$. Repeating this argument,
284	  %  we see that there exists a smaller neighbourhood $V$ of $s$ in which
285	  %  there is a right-exact sequence
286	  %  ${\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus m} \stackrel{\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus e}
287	  %  \stackrel{\phi}{\to} \F \to 0$.
288	  %  Let $I_e\subset {\mathcal O}_V$ be the ideal sheaf
289	  %  formed by the entries of the $e\times m$ matrix
290	  %  $(\psi_{i,j})$ [...].
291	  %  Let $V_e$ be the closed subscheme of $V$ defined by $I_e$.
292	  %  For any morphism of schemes
293	  %  $f: T\to V$, the pull-back sequence [...]
294	  %  is exact, by right-exactness of tensor products.
295	  %  Hence the pull-back $f^*\F$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_T$-module of
296	  %  rank $e$ if and only if $f^*\psi =0$, that is,
297	  %  $f$ factors via the subscheme $V_e\hra V$ defined by the vanishing of all
298	  %  entries $\psi_{i,j}$.
299	  %  Thus we have proved assertions \textbf{(i)} and \textbf{(ii)} of the theorem.
300	  %
301	  %  As the rank of the matrix $(\psi_{i,j})$ is lower semi-continuous,
302	  %  it follows that the function $e$ is upper semi-continuous, which
303	  %  proves the assertion \textbf{(iii)} of the theorem, completing its proof
304	  %  when $n=0$."
305	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (special case $n=0$ of the main theorem).}
306	  Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme and let $\F$ be a coherent $\OO_S$-module.
307	  For each integer $e \ge 0$, there exists a locally-closed subscheme
308	  $S_e \hookrightarrow S$ whose underlying set is
309	  $\{s \in S : \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s = e\}$. The family $\{S_e\}_e$ is a
310	  finite, mutually disjoint, set-theoretically covering stratification of $S$,
311	  and a morphism $f : T \to S$ pulls back $\F$ to a locally-free
312	  $\OO_T$-module of rank $e$ if and only if $f$ factors through $S_e$.  The
313	  function $s \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s$ is upper-semicontinuous on $S$.
314	\end{lemma}
315	
316	\begin{proof}
317	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
318	  By [Nitsure], \S4 (verbatim above): for $s \in S$, Nakayama produces a
319	  neighbourhood $V$ of $s$ and a right-exact local presentation
320	  $\OO_V^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\psi} \OO_V^{\oplus e} \to \F|_V \to 0$ where
321	  $e = \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s$.  The closed subscheme $V_e \hookrightarrow V$
322	  defined by the ideal sheaf generated by the matrix entries of $\psi$
323	  represents the locus where $\F$ becomes locally free of rank $e$: by
324	  right-exactness of tensor product, a base change $f : T \to V$ has
325	  $f^* \F$ locally free of rank $e$ iff $f^* \psi = 0$, iff $f$ factors
326	  through $V_e$.  Glueing the $V_e$ over the cover of $S$ by such
327	  neighbourhoods (and intersecting with $V \setminus \bigcup_{e' > e} V_{e'}$
328	  to get the strictly-equal-to-$e$ stratum) produces the locally-closed
329	  $S_e$. Upper-semicontinuity of $e$ follows because the rank of $\psi$ is
330	  lower-semicontinuous.
331	\end{proof}
332	
333	\begin{lemma}
334	[Noetherian induction reduction to the flat case]
335	  \label{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}
336	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat, thm:generic_flatness}
337	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', General case
338	  % opening paragraphs (read from
339	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1888-L1929).
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
341	  % "\textbf{General case: } We now allow the integer $n$ to be arbitrary.
342	  %  The idea of the proof is as follows:
343	  %  We show the existence of a stratification
344	  %  of $S$ which is a `g.c.d.' of the flattening
345	  %  stratifications for direct images $\pi_*\F(i)$ for all $i \ge N$ for some
346	  %  integer $N$ [...].
347	  %
348	  %  As $S$ is noetherian, it is a finite union of irreducible components,
349	  %  and these are closed in $S$.
350	  %  Let $Y$ be an irreducible component of $S$, and let $U$ be the
351	  %  non-empty open subset of $Y$ which consists of all points which do not
352	  %  lie on any other irreducible component of $S$.
353	  %  Let $U$ be given the reduced subscheme
354	  %  structure. Note that this makes $U$ an integral scheme, which is a
355	  %  locally closed subscheme of $S$.
356	  %  By Theorem \ref{generic flatness} on generic flatness,
357	  %  $U$ has a non-empty open subscheme $V$ such that
358	  %  the restriction of $\F$ to $\PP^n_V$ is flat over ${\mathcal O}_V$.
359	  %  Now repeating the argument with $S$ replaced by its reduced
360	  %  closed subscheme $S-V$,
361	  %  it follows by noetherian induction on $S$ that there exist
362	  %  finitely many reduced, locally closed, mutually disjoint subschemes
363	  %  $V_i$ of $S$ such that set-theoretically
364	  %  $|S|$ is the union of the $|V_i|$ and
365	  %  the restriction of $\F$ to $\PP^n_{V_i}$ is flat over ${\mathcal O}_{V_i}$.
366	  %  As each $V_i$ is a noetherian scheme, and as the Hilbert polynomials
367	  %  are locally constant for a flat family of sheaves, it follows
368	  %  that only finitely many polynomials occur in $V_i$
369	  %  in the family of Hilbert polynomials $P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))$
370	  %  as $s$ varies over points of $V_i$. This allows us to conclude
371	  %  the following:
372	  %
373	  %  \textbf{(A) } Only finitely many distinct Hilbert polynomials
374	  %  $P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))$ occur, as $s$
375	  %  varies over all of $S$."
376	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (general case, reduction by Noetherian induction).}
377	  Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $\pi : \PP^n_S \to S$ the relative projective
378	  space, and $\F$ a coherent sheaf on $\PP^n_S$.  Then there exist finitely many
379	  reduced, locally-closed, pairwise disjoint subschemes $V_i \hookrightarrow S$
380	  whose set-theoretic union is $|S|$ and such that $\F|_{\PP^n_{V_i}}$ is
381	  flat over $\OO_{V_i}$ for each $i$. Consequently, only finitely many
382	  Hilbert polynomials $P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))$ occur as $s$ varies
383	  over $S$.
384	\end{lemma}
385	
386	\begin{proof}
387	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness}
388	  By [Nitsure], \S4: $S$ being noetherian, decompose $|S| = \bigcup Y_j$ into
389	  finitely many irreducible components, each closed.  For an irreducible
390	  component $Y$, let $U \subseteq Y$ be the open subset not lying on any other
391	  component, given the reduced subscheme structure (an integral, locally-closed
392	  subscheme of $S$).  By Theorem~\ref{thm:generic_flatness} applied to
393	  $\F|_{\PP^n_U}$, there is a non-empty open $V \subseteq U$ over which $\F$ is
394	  flat.  Replace $S$ by the reduced closed complement $S \setminus V$ and
395	  repeat. Noetherianity of $S$ terminates the induction in finitely many
396	  steps, producing the $V_i$.  On each $V_i$ the Hilbert polynomial is locally
397	  constant (flat families have locally constant Hilbert polynomial), hence
398	  takes finitely many values on the noetherian (hence quasi-compact) scheme
399	  $V_i$; the finite union of finite sets is finite.
400	\end{proof}
401	
402	\begin{lemma}
403	[Assembly of the strata by Noetherian induction]
404	  \label{lem:noetherian_induction_strata}
405	  \uses{lem:flat_locus_open, lem:nonflat_locus_proper}
406	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Assembly via
407	  % direct images $E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)$ (read from
408	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1992-L2090).
409	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
410	  % "Let $\pi : \PP^n_S \to S$ denote the projection. Consider the
411	  %  coherent sheaves $E_0, \ldots, E_n$ on $S$, defined by
412	  %  $E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\mbox{ for } i=0,\ldots,n.$
413	  %  By applying the special case of of the theorem (where the relative dimension
414	  %  $n$ of $\PP^n_S$ is $0$) to the sheaf $E_0$ on $\PP^0_S=S$, we get
415	  %  a stratification $(W_{e_0})$ of $S$
416	  %  indexed by integers $e_0$, such that for any morphism
417	  %  $f: T\to S$ the pull-back $f^*E_0$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_T$-module
418	  %  of rank $e_0$ if and only if $f$ factors via $W_{e_0} \hra S$.
419	  %  Next, for each stratum $W_{e_0}$, we take the
420	  %  flattening stratification $(W_{e_0,e_1})$ for $E_1|_{W_{e_0}}$, and so on.
421	  %  Thus in $n+1$ steps, we obtain finitely many
422	  %  locally closed subschemes
423	  %  $W_{e_0,\ldots,e_n} \subset S$
424	  %  such that for any morphism $f:T\to S$ the pull-back
425	  %  $f^*E_i$ for $i=0,\ldots,n$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_T$-modules of
426	  %  of constant rank $e_i$ if and only if $f$ factors
427	  %  via $W_{e_0,\ldots,e_n} \hra S$. [...]
428	  %  This shows that at any point $s\in W_f$, the Hilbert polynomial
429	  %  $P_s(m)$ equals $f$. The desired locally closed subscheme $S_f \subset S$,
430	  %  whose existence is asserted by the theorem,
431	  %  will turn out to be a certain closed subscheme $S_f \subset W_f$ whose
432	  %  underlying subset is all of $|W_f|$. [...]
433	  %  As $S_f$ is the flattening stratification for $\pi_*\F(p)$,
434	  %  the property (iii) of the theorem follows from the corresponding property in
435	  %  the case $n=0$, applied to the sheaf $\pi_*\F(p)$ on $S$."
436	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (assembly step combining the $n=0$ special case
437	  applied to direct images $\pi_*\F(N+i)$).}
438	  Let $S$, $\pi : \PP^n_S \to S$, $\F$ be as in
439	  Theorem~\ref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}.  There exists an integer
440	  $N \ge 0$ such that for every $s \in S$ the higher cohomologies
441	  $H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))$ vanish for all $r \ge 1$, $m \ge N$, and the base-change
442	  homomorphism $(\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))$ is an isomorphism
443	  for $m \ge N$, $s \in S$.  Iterating the $n=0$ stratification
444	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open}) on the coherent sheaves $E_i := \pi_* \F(N+i)$
445	  for $i = 0, \dots, n$ produces a finite collection of locally-closed
446	  subschemes $W_{e_0, \dots, e_n} \subseteq S$ indexed by tuples
447	  $(e_0, \dots, e_n) \in \Z^{n+1}$. Re-indexing each tuple by the unique
448	  numerical polynomial $f \in \Q[\lambda]$ of degree $\le n$ with
449	  $f(N+i) = e_i$ gives a finite stratification $\{W_f\}$ of $S$, and the
450	  locally-closed subschemes $S_f \subseteq W_f$ of
451	  Theorem~\ref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} are obtained by further
452	  intersecting with the closed-subscheme conditions that
453	  $\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}$ be locally free of rank $f(N+i)$ for all $i \ge 0$
454	  (terminating by Noetherianity).
455	\end{lemma}
456	
457	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: full proof is the long body of [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem
458	% ``flattening stratification'', spanning L1844-L2090 (~250 lines: special
459	% case, Noetherian induction, statements (A)/(B)/(C), then the assembly via
460	% direct images and the closed-subscheme construction inside each $W_f$).
461	% Verbatim retrieved per-sub-lemma above; the structural restatement below in
462	% project notation is the canonical Nitsure-style restated proof.
463	\begin{proof}
464	  \uses{lem:flat_locus_open, lem:nonflat_locus_proper, thm:generic_flatness}
465	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}, the family of Hilbert polynomials
466	  $\{P_s\}$ on $S$ is finite, and there is a finite stratification
467	  $\{V_i\}$ of $S$ on each of which $\F$ is flat with locally-constant
468	  Hilbert polynomial.  Apply semi-continuity (cohomology and base change,
469	  [Hartshorne], III.12 / [Stacks Project], tag 02KH for the $H^0$-version) on
470	  the noetherian $V_i$ to obtain a uniform integer $N_1$ with
471	  $R^r \pi_* \F(m) = 0$ for $r \ge 1$, $m \ge N_1$, and $H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m)) = 0$
472	  for all $s \in S$, $r \ge 1$, $m \ge N_1$ (statement (B) of Nitsure's proof).
473	  Choose $r_i \ge N_1$ on each $V_i$ such that the base-change comparison
474	  $(\pi_* \F(m))|_{V_i} \to (\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m)$ is an isomorphism for
475	  $m \ge r_i$; composing with the cohomology-and-base-change isomorphism
476	  $((\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m))|_s \xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))$ for
477	  the flat family on $V_i$ yields uniform $N = \max r_i$ such that
478	  $(\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))$ is an isomorphism for $m \ge N$
479	  and all $s \in S$ (statement (C)).
480	
481	  Apply Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open} sequentially to the coherent sheaves
482	  $E_i = \pi_* \F(N+i)$ on $S$ for $i = 0, \dots, n$: $E_0$ produces a
483	  stratification $\{W_{e_0}\}$ of $S$; on each $W_{e_0}$ apply the lemma to
484	  $E_1|_{W_{e_0}}$ to refine to $\{W_{e_0, e_1}\}$, and continue. After $n+1$
485	  steps the $W_{e_0, \dots, e_n}$ form a finite locally-closed stratification
486	  of $S$ universal for the property ``each $E_i$ pulls back to a locally-free
487	  $\OO_T$-module of constant rank $e_i$''.  Re-index $(e_0, \dots, e_n)$ by the
488	  unique numerical polynomial $f$ of degree $\le n$ with $f(N+i) = e_i$ to get
489	  the family $\{W_f\}$ indexed by polynomials.  At any $s \in W_f$, statement
490	  (C) and vanishing (B) force $P_s = f$ (since $P_s$ has degree $\le n$ and is
491	  determined by its values at $N, N+1, \dots, N+n$).
492	
493	  The Hilbert-polynomial-coincidence locus $|W_f|$ may have non-flat scheme
494	  structure; refine to $S_f \subseteq W_f$ by intersecting with the
495	  closed-subscheme conditions ``$\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}$ is locally-free of rank
496	  $f(N+i)$'' for all $i \ge 0$.  These conditions are cut out by an increasing
497	  chain of coherent ideal sheaves $I_0 \subseteq I_0 + I_1 \subseteq \cdots$ on
498	  $W_f$; Noetherianity terminates the chain in finitely many steps, defining a
499	  coherent ideal sheaf $I$ and a closed subscheme $S_f \subseteq W_f$ with
500	  $|S_f| = |W_f|$ on which $\pi_* \F(N+i)$ is locally-free of rank $f(N+i)$
501	  for all $i \ge 0$. The base-change-without-flatness lemma (cf.\ [Nitsure],
502	  \S3) yields some $N' \ge N$ such that for $i \ge N'$,
503	  $(\pi_* \F(i))|_{S_f} \to (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)$ is an isomorphism, hence
504	  the graded $A$-module $\bigoplus_{i \ge N'} (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)$ is
505	  locally-free on $S_f$, hence $\F_{S_f}$ is flat over $S_f$. The
506	  closure-of-strata property (iii) reduces to the corresponding property in
507	  the $n=0$ case for $\pi_* \F(p)$ on $S$ at any $p \ge N$ separating all
508	  Hilbert polynomials.
509	\end{proof}
510	
511	\section{Universal property of the strata}
512	\label{sec:flatstrat_universal}
513	
514	\begin{theorem}
515	[Universal property of the flattening stratification]
516	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_universal}
517	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification_universal}
518	  \uses{thm:flattening_stratification_exists, def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
519	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', part (ii)
520	  % universal property
521	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1826-L1834).
522	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
523	  % "\textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
524	  %  Let $S'=\coprod S_f$ be the coproduct of the $S_f$, and let
525	  %  $i:S'\to S$ be the morphism induced by the inclusions
526	  %  $S_f\hra S$. Then the sheaf $i^*(\F)$ on $\PP^n_{S'}$
527	  %  is flat over $S'$. Moreover, $i:S'\to S$ has the universal property that

531	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial $f$."
532	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (part (ii) of the main theorem); cf.\ [Stacks
533	  Project], tag 052H.}
534	  Let $S$, $\F$, and $\{S_f\}_{f \in I}$ be as in
535	  Theorem~\ref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}.  Set
536	  $S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f$ with the coproduct morphism
537	  $i : S' \to S$ assembled from the locally-closed inclusions.  Then $i^* \F$
538	  is flat over $S'$, and the pair $(S', i)$ is universal: for any morphism
539	  $\varphi : T \to S$ of noetherian schemes (one may take $T$ arbitrary, the
540	  Noetherian hypothesis on $S$ being preserved), the pullback
541	  $\varphi^* \F$ on $\PP^n_T$ is $T$-flat if and only if $\varphi$ factors
542	  uniquely through $i$. In particular each $S_f$ is uniquely determined by $f$.
543	\end{theorem}

1	\chapter{The relative Picard functor and its \'etale sheafification}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.1.c sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It upgrades the set-valued
7	% relative Picard presheaf $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ of
8	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) to its abelian-group-valued
9	% refinement and then to its \'etale sheafification
10	% $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim$. The chapter is gated on
11	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b, pullback functor + set-valued
12	% quotient on disk) and on \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, the
13	% relative-spectrum machinery underlying any concrete Lean-side computation).
14	% It \emph{gates} A.2.c (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) since
15	% Grothendieck's existence theorem represents precisely the \'etale-sheafified
16	% functor (cf.\ [Kleiman], \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
17	% $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$.''). It is \emph{not} responsible for representability
18	% (that is A.2.c) and not responsible for the identity-component $\Pic^0_{C/k}$
19	% (that is A.3).
20	
21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the
23	% quotient $\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)$ that the set-valued chapter
24	% A.1.b deliberately suppressed. The structure is canonical: $\Pic(C \times_k T)$
25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image $\pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ is a subgroup
26	% because $\pi_T^*$ is a group homomorphism, and the quotient is abelian. Second,
27	% \emph{sheafify} the resulting group-valued presheaf
28	% $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab$ in the \'etale topology; the
29	% sheafification is the functor Kleiman \S 4 represents. The \'etale-sheaf
30	% refinement is necessary because, by Kleiman \S 2, $\Pic_X$ is never a Zariski
31	% (let alone \'etale) sheaf; the smallest \'etale sheaf below $\Pic_X$ is the
32	% one that captures line bundles that are trivializable \emph{\'etale-locally},
33	% which is the right notion for Grothendieck--Mumford--Kleiman representability.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve $C$ over
39	$k$. As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	$\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$ for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	$k$-scheme $T$ and
42	$\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)$ for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~\ref{sec:relpic_group}
52	and \S~\ref{sec:relpic_groupvalued}) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	$\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab$ on the category of $k$-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~\ref{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	$\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim$ in the \'etale topology on
58	$(\Sch/k)^{op}$, and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group $\Pic(X)$ of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of $[\mathcal{L}]$
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	$\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)$ of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	$\mathcal{O}_X$-modules sends $\mathcal{O}_T$ to $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
75	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
76	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
77	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
78	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
92	  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on $\Pic$).}
97	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let $T$ be a
98	  $k$-scheme. The Picard group $\Pic(C \times_k T)$ is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of $\Pic(C \times_k T)$, and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly $H_T$.
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
124	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
125	  The abelian-group structure on $\Pic(C \times_k T)$ under tensor product is
126	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
127	  $\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'$, the tensor product
128	  $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'$ is again
129	  invertible, the structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$ is the unit, and
130	  the dual $\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})$ is the
131	  inverse. Pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules sends
132	  $\mathcal{O}_T$ to $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$ (the structure sheaf is
133	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
134	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
135	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
136	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore $\pi_T^*$
137	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
138	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
139	
140	  The image $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ is then a subgroup. The quotient set
141	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T$ of
142	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
143	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
144	  abelian quotient): for $\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)$, set
145	  $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']$;
146	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
147	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T$ is by construction a
148	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly $H_T$.
149	\end{proof}
150

150	
151	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
152	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
153	
154	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
155	group-valued presheaf.
156	
157	\begin{definition}[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection,
161	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined, lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
162	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
163	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)
164	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
166	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
167	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
168	  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
169	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
170	  % topologies  by
171	  %     $$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
172	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
173	  %  \end{dfn}"
174	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
175	  Def.~df:Pfs.}
176	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
177	  \emph{relative Picard presheaf} of $C$ over $k$ is the functor
178	  \[
179	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \Ab,
180	    \qquad
181	    T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T),
182	  \]
183	  where the right-hand side is the quotient abelian group of
184	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the structure morphisms are induced by
185	  the pullback maps $g^\sharp$ of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} (which restrict
186	  to group homomorphisms on the quotient because the source square commutes
187	  with all pullback functors, see the proof below). In Kleiman's notation with
188	  $X = C$ and $S = \Spec k$, this is exactly $\Pic_{X/S}$ of df:Pfs.
189	\end{definition}
190	
191	\begin{lemma}[Naturality respects the group structure]
192	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
194	  \uses{def:rel_pic_sharp, lem:pullback_compose, lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid,
195	        thm:pullback_natural}
196	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
197	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
198	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
199	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
200	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
201	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
204	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
205	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
206	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
207	  %  [\dots]
208	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
209	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
210	  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$"
211	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
212	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
213	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
214	  For a morphism $g : T' \to T$ of $k$-schemes, the induced set map
215	  $g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')$ of
216	  \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a homomorphism of abelian groups with respect
217	  to the structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the identity and
218	  composition laws $\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}$ and
219	  $(g \circ h)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ g^\sharp$ hold as identities of group
220	  homomorphisms.
221	\end{lemma}
222	
223	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
224	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
225	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument
226	% is a direct unfolding of the pullback of modules.
227	\begin{proof}
228	  \uses{def:rel_pic_sharp, lem:pullback_compose, lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid,
229	        thm:pullback_natural}
230	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map $g^\sharp$ is defined by
231	  $[\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]$, where
232	  $g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T$. Pullback
233	  of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along $g_C$ preserves tensor products and the
234	  structure sheaf, hence the map
235	  $g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')$ on Picard groups is
236	  itself an abelian-group homomorphism. By
237	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, the quotient map
238	  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is a group homomorphism, and
239	  $g_C^*$ takes $H_T$ into $H_{T'}$ by \cref{lem:pullback_compose}; therefore
240	  $g^\sharp$ is a group homomorphism on the quotient. The identity and
241	  composition laws are inherited from $\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}$ and
242	  $(g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*$ on $\mathcal{O}$-module pullback.
243	\end{proof}
244	
245	\begin{theorem}[$\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ is a group-valued presheaf]
246	  \label{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}
247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
248	  \uses{def:rel_pic_sharp, lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial,
249	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid, thm:pullback_natural}
250	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
251	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (relative Picard functor as a functor into
252	  % abelian groups)
253	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
254	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
255	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
256	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
257	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
258	  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$"
259	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
260	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.}
261	  The assignment $T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ of \cref{def:rel_pic_sharp},
262	  together with the structure maps $g \mapsto g^\sharp$ of
263	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, defines a presheaf of abelian groups
264	  \[
265	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\;
266	                                     \AddCommGroup
267	  \]
268	  on the category of $k$-schemes.
269	\end{theorem}
270	
271	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with the prior theorems, Kleiman \S 2 does not supply
272	% a separate proof body --- the presheaf-of-abelian-groups property is part of
273	% the definition df:aPf + df:Pfs. The project-side proof assembles the lemmas
274	% above; no external proof is being translated.
275	\begin{proof}
276	  \uses{def:rel_pic_sharp, lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial,
277	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid, thm:pullback_natural}
278	  The object map is well defined by
279	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}: for each $k$-scheme $T$, the value
280	  $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is an abelian group. The morphism map
281	  $g \mapsto g^\sharp$ is a group homomorphism by
282	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, and satisfies the identity and
283	  composition laws by the same lemma. These data assemble into a functor
284	  $(\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup$, i.e.\ a presheaf of abelian groups on
285	  $(\Sch/k)$.
286	\end{proof}
287	
288	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
289	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
290	
291	The presheaf $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
292	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a $k$-scheme
293	$T$ carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to $C \times_k T$ becomes
294	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
295	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
296	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
297	$\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
298	this sheaf $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
299	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
300	
301	\begin{definition}[\'Etale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf]
302	  \label{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
304	  \uses{def:rel_pic_sharp, thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}
305	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
306	  % Definition df:Pfs --- the second half of df:Pfs names the \'etale sheaf
307	  % associated to the relative Picard functor
308	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318
309	  %  and the surrounding paragraph L1320-L1328).
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
311	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
312	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
313	  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
314	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
315	  % topologies  by
316	  %     $$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
317	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
318	  %  \end{dfn}
319	  %  [\dots]
320	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
321	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
322	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
323	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
324	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
325	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$, as well as to the
326	  %  relative Picard functor $\Pic_{X/S}$."
327	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
328	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale-sheaf notation $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$) and the
329	  associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following df:Pfs.}
330	  Let $C/k$ be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
331	  \emph{\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor} is the sheafification of
332	  the presheaf $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) in the
333	  global \'etale topology on $(\Sch/k)$:
334	  \[
335	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \;\;:=\;\;
336	       \bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\bigr)^{\sim_{\et}}.
337	  \]
338	  Equivalently, $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ is the unique (up to canonical
339	  isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the \'etale site of $\Sch/k$
340	  equipped with a presheaf morphism $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to
341	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ which is universal among presheaf morphisms from
342	  $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ to \'etale sheaves of abelian groups. In Kleiman's
343	  notation this is $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ with $X = C$ and $S = \Spec k$. When $k$
344	  is algebraically closed and $T = \Spec k'$ for an algebraically closed field
345	  extension $k'/k$, the natural map
346	  $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$ is a bijection (Kleiman
347	  Exercise ex:Alr, L1357--L1361).
348	\end{definition}
349	
350	\begin{theorem}[\'Etale sheafification preserves the abelian-group structure]
351	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}
352	  \uses{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}
353	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
354	  % Def.~df:Pfs --- the \'etale-sheafified functor $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ is named
355	  % alongside the abelian-group-valued $\Pic_{X/S}$ in the same definition,
356	  % i.e.\ the sheafification lives in the same target category
357	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
358	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
359	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
360	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
361	  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
362	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
363	  % topologies  by
364	  %     $$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
365	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
366	  %  \end{dfn}"
367	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
368	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale sheaf $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$ is named together with the
369	  abelian-group-valued relative Picard functor, meaning the sheaf is taken in
370	  the category of abelian groups).}
371	  The \'etale sheafification
372	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ of \cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification} is a
373	  sheaf with values in the category of abelian groups: the canonical morphism
374	  $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ is a homomorphism of group
375	  presheaves, and for each $k$-scheme $T$ the value
376	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$ is naturally an abelian group whose structure
377	  maps make $T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)$ a presheaf
378	  $(\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup$.
379	\end{theorem}
380	
381	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a self-contained proof of
382	% this auxiliary statement --- it is the standard fact that the sheafification
383	% of a presheaf of abelian groups in a Grothendieck topology is again a
384	% presheaf of abelian groups (equivalently, the forgetful functor
385	% $\AddCommGroup \to \Set$ preserves filtered colimits, and the
386	% plus-construction is built from such colimits). The Lean side appeals to
387	% Mathlib's $\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}$ machinery.
388	\begin{proof}
389	  \uses{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification, thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}
390	  The \'etale topology on $(\Sch/k)$ is a Grothendieck topology, and the
391	  forgetful functor $\AddCommGroup \to \Set$ preserves and reflects the
392	  filtered colimits used by the plus-construction. Therefore the
393	  sheafification of a presheaf $\mathcal{F} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup$
394	  in the underlying \'etale site agrees, after the forgetful functor to sets,
395	  with the sheafification of the underlying set-valued presheaf
396	  $U \circ \mathcal{F}$; equivalently, the sheaf
397	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} = (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}$ is canonically a
398	  sheaf of abelian groups, the canonical morphism
399	  $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ is a homomorphism of group
400	  presheaves, and the restriction maps are group homomorphisms. This is a
401	  standard consequence of the plus-construction with values in an algebraic
402	  category; on the Lean side it is the Mathlib pattern
403	  $\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}$ applied with target category
404	  $\AddCommGroup$.
405	\end{proof}
406	
407	\section{Lean encoding}
408	\label{sec:relpic_lean_encoding}
409	
410	The Lean target file is the new
411	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean}, sibling to
412	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
413	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}) and
414	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
415	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
416	Lean targets as follows.
417	\begin{itemize}
418	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
419	    (\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}) is the abelian-group instance on the
420	    quotient set built in
421	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
422	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). Mathlib's
423	    \texttt{QuotientAddGroup} machinery on a normal subgroup of an abelian
424	    group is the backbone; the project-side work is to certify that
425	    $\pi_T^*$ is a group homomorphism (one extra
426	    \texttt{LinearMap.map\_mul}--style line on tensor product preservation).
427	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
428	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}) is the group-valued presheaf
429	    $T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)$. The Lean form is a functor
430	    $(\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup$ built from the data of
431	    \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} +
432	    \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
433	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
434	    (\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}) packages the structure-map
435	    homomorphism property: the set map $g^\sharp$ of
436	    \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a group homomorphism. On the Lean side
437	    this is a one-step strengthening of the existing set-valued naturality
438	    proof.
439	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
440	    (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) wraps the previous three lemmas as a
441	    functor instance into $\AddCommGroup$.
442	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
443	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}) is the \'etale sheafification
444	    of \texttt{PicSharp}. The Lean form is
445	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.Sheafify} applied to the
446	    \'etale topology on $\Sch/k$ with target category $\AddCommGroup$.
447	    Mathlib provides the abelian-group-valued sheafification API
448	    (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}) for any concrete sheafifiable
449	    presheaf and any target with the required filtered-colimit + finite-limit
450	    structure; \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure} reduces the
451	    statement to that pattern. \textbf{Verification flag.} The exact Mathlib
452	    declaration name and the input-shape of the \'etale-Grothendieck-topology
453	    instance on $\Sch/k$ are not pinned in this chapter; the prover should
454	    consult the current Mathlib snapshot (or open a follow-up if the API is
455	    not yet wired to $\AddCommGroup$). If the abelian-group-valued
456	    sheafification is absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, the
457	    project-side sub-build is to combine the set-valued sheafification with
458	    Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGroup} forgetful functor.
459	\end{itemize}
460	
461	The relationship with \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} is captured by
462	the slogan: the FGA Picard scheme of A.2.c represents
463	$\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (Kleiman \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
464	$\Pic_{(X/S)\et}$''), \emph{not} $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ directly. The \'etale
465	sheafification step of this chapter is precisely what makes the
466	representability theorem in A.2.c well-posed.
467

2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}

6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
8	% $\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ -- a projective $S$-scheme representing the
9	% functor of $T$-flat coherent quotients of $E_T$ on $X_T = X \times_S T$ with
10	% Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$ on every fiber. The Quot construction is the
11	% \emph{existence engine} behind the relative Picard functor: A.2.c
12	% (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) wires this chapter together with
13	% the relative Picard functor (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, A.1.c)
14	% through the Abel map $\Div_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$, which factors
15	% through the Hilbert scheme $\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$ -- the
16	% case $E = \mathcal{O}_X$ of the Quot functor. This chapter is gated on
17	% A.2.a (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}, written this iter) and
18	% A.1.a (\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}, on disk).
19	%
20	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The construction embeds Quot as a
21	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
22	% $\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))$.
23	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
24	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
25	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $\pi : X \to S$ a
36	projective morphism, $L$ a relatively very ample line bundle on $X$ over $S$,
37	and $E$ a coherent sheaf on $X$. The Quot construction packages the
38	$T$-flat coherent quotients $E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}$ of the
39	pull-back $E_T$ to $X_T = X \times_S T$, modulo the equivalence
40	$\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle$ defined by
41	$\ker(q) = \ker(q')$. Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	$\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$
43	(constant on connected $T$ by flatness; see~\S\ref{sec:hilbert_polynomial})
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$, each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective $S$-scheme.
46	
47	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
48	% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
49	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500 +
50	% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
51	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
52	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
53	% the verbatim source-quote in \S\ref{sec:hilbert_polynomial} is taken from
54	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
55	% reference).
56	
57	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
58	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
59	
60	\begin{definition}[Hilbert polynomial]
61	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
62	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
63	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
64	  % Polynomials" (read from
65	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
66	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
67	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
68	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
69	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
70	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
71	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
72	  %  the function
73	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
74	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
75	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
76	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
77	  %  is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
78	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
79	  %
80	  %  Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
81	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
82	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
83	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
84	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
85	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
86	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
87	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$.
88	  %  If $\F$ is flat over $S$ then the function $s\mapsto {\Phi}_s$
89	  %  from the set of points of $S$ to the polynomial ring $\Q[\lambda]$ is
90	  %  known to be locally constant on $S$."
91	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
92	  III.5.2.}
93	  Let $X \to S$ be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let $L$
94	  be a line bundle on $X$. For a coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$ whose
95	  schematic support is proper over $S$ and a point $s \in S$, the
96	  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of $\mathcal{F}$ at $s$ relative to $L$} is the
97	  polynomial $\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$ characterised by
98	  \[
99	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
100	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
101	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
102	  \]
103	  (a polynomial in $m$ by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
104	  properness). When $\mathcal{F}$ is $S$-flat the function
105	  $s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}$ is locally constant on $S$, so $\Phi$ is
106	  well-defined on connected components.
107	\end{definition}
108	
109	\section{The Quot functor}
110	\label{sec:quot_functor}
111	
112	\begin{definition}[Quot functor]
113	  \label{def:quot_functor}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
115	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial}
116	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
117	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
118	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
119	  % + L481-L498).
120	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
121	  % "Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme and let $X\to S$ be a finite type
122	  %  scheme over it. Let $E$ be a coherent sheaf on $X$.
123	  %  Let $Sch_S$ denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
124	  %  over $S$. For any $T\to S$ in $Sch_S$, a \textbf{family of quotients of $E$
125	  %  parametrised by $T$} will mean
126	  %  a pair $(\F,q)$ consisting of
127	  %
128	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
129	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
130	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
131	  %
132	  %  (ii) a surjective ${\mathcal O}_{X_T}$-linear homomorphism of sheaves
133	  %  $q:E_T  \to \F$ where $E_T$ is the pull-back of $E$ under
134	  %  the projection $X_T \to X$.
135	  %
136	  %  Two such families $(\F,q)$ and $(\F,q)$ parametrised by $T$ will
137	  %  be regarded as equivalent if $\ker(q) = \ker(q')$),
138	  %  and $\langle \F, q \rangle$ will denote
139	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
140	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
141	  %  the pull-back of $\langle \F, q \rangle$ under an $S$-morphism $T'\to T$ is
142	  %  well-defined, which gives
143	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
144	  %  $\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets$ under which
145	  %  $$T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}$$
146	  %  [...]
147	  %  This shows that the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}$ naturally decomposes
148	  %  as a co-product
149	  %  $$\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$$
150	  %  where for any polynomial ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$, the functor

150	  %  where for any polynomial ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$, the functor
151	  %  $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ associates
152	  %  to any $T$ the set of all equivalence classes of
153	  %  families $\langle \F,q \rangle$ such that at each $t\in T$ the
154	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction $\F_t$, calculated
155	  %  using the pull-back of $L$, is ${\Phi}$."
156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
157	  Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $\pi : X \to S$ a finite-type morphism,
158	  $L$ a line bundle on $X$, $E$ a coherent sheaf on $X$, and
159	  $\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$. The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
160	  polynomial $\Phi$}
161	  \[
162	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
163	  \]
164	  sends an $S$-scheme $T \to S$ to the set of equivalence classes
165	  $\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle$ of pairs $(\mathcal{F}, q)$ where
166	  \begin{enumerate}
167	    \item $\mathcal{F}$ is a coherent sheaf on $X_T = X \times_S T$ whose
168	      schematic support is proper over $T$ and which is flat over $T$;
169	    \item $q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}$ is a surjective
170	      $\mathcal{O}_{X_T}$-linear homomorphism, where $E_T$ is the pull-back
171	      of $E$ along $X_T \to X$;
172	    \item for every $t \in T$ the Hilbert polynomial of $\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}$
173	      with respect to $L|_{X_t}$ (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
174	      $\Phi$.
175	  \end{enumerate}
176	  Two pairs $(\mathcal{F}, q)$ and $(\mathcal{F}', q')$ are equivalent iff
177	  $\ker(q) = \ker(q')$. On morphisms $f : T' \to T$ over $S$, the functor
178	  acts by pulling back the quotient along $X_{T'} \to X_T$ (well-defined
179	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
180	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
181	  $\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}$.
182	\end{definition}
183	
184	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
185	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
186	
187	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ as a
188	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over $S$. Since Mathlib carries
189	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
190	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
191	
192	\begin{definition}[Grassmannian scheme]
193	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
194	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
195	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
196	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
197	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
198	  % + L773-L850).
199	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
200	  % "For any integers $r\ge d\ge 1$, an explicit construction
201	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
202	  %  $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$, together with the tautological
203	  %  quotient $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ where \
204	  %  $\U$ is a rank $d$ locally free sheaf on $\grass(r,d)$,
205	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
206	  %  Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
207	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
208	  %  contravariant functor
209	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
210	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
211	  %  set of all equivalence classes
212	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
213	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
214	  %  [...]
215	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if $S$ is a scheme and
216	  %  $E$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_S$-module of rank $r$,
217	  %  the functor $\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}$
218	  %  on all $S$-schemes which
219	  %  by definition associates to any $T$
220	  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients
221	  %  $q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,
222	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
223	  %  is denoted by $\grass(E,d)$ and is called the rank $d$
224	  %  relative Grassmannian of $E$ over $S$."
225	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
226	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
227	  Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme and let $V$ be a locally free
228	  $\mathcal{O}_S$-module of rank $r$. For an integer $1 \le d \le r$, the
229	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-$d$ quotients of $V$} is the functor
230	  \[
231	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
232	    \qquad
233	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
234	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
235	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
236	  \]
237	  with equivalence $\ker(q) = \ker(q')$. Concretely $\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
238	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}$ (the Quot functor for $X = S$, $E = V$,
239	  constant Hilbert polynomial $\Phi = d$).
240	\end{definition}
241	
242	\begin{theorem}[Representability of the Grassmannian]
243	  \label{thm:grassmannian_representable}
244	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable}
245	  \uses{def:grassmannian_scheme}
246	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
247	  % affine patches" through "Projective embedding"
248	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
249	  % L798-L930).
250	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
251	  % "For any integers $r\ge d\ge 1$, the Grassmannian scheme
252	  %  $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$, together with the tautological
253	  %  quotient $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ where \
254	  %  $\U$ is a rank $d$ locally free sheaf on $\grass(r,d)$,
255	  %  can be explicitly constructed as follows. [...]
256	  %  the schemes
257	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
258	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
259	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
260	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
261	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
262	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
263	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$.
264	  %  [...]
265	  %  We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
266	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
267	  %  [...]
268	  %  As $\U$ is given by the transition functions $g_{I,J}$ described above,
269	  %  the determinant line bundle
270	  %  $\det(\U)$ is given by the transition functions
271	  %  $\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)$.
272	  %  [...]
273	  %  We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections $\sigma_I$ form
274	  %  a linear system which is base point free and separates points
275	  %  relative to $\spec \Z$, and so gives an embedding of
276	  %  $\grass(r,d)$ into $\PP^m_{\Z}$ where $m = {r\choose d}-1$.
277	  %  This is a closed embedding by the properness of
278	  %  $\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$. In particular, $\det(\U)$
279	  %  is a relatively very ample line bundle on $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$."
280	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
281	  Explained Ch.~5).}
282	  Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $V$ a locally free $\mathcal{O}_S$-module
283	  of rank $r$, and $1 \le d \le r$. The functor $\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)$ of
284	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
285	  $S$-scheme $\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S$ of relative dimension $d(r-d)$,
286	  equipped with a tautological quotient
287	  $\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}$ of rank $d$. The determinant
288	  line bundle $\det(\mathcal{U})$ is relatively very ample, and the
289	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
290	  $\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
291	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)$ -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
292	\end{theorem}
293	
294	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
295	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
296	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
297	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
298	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
299	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
300	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
301	\begin{proof}
302	  \uses{def:grassmannian_scheme}
303	  Over $S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}$ and $V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}$ the
304	  Grassmannian $\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)$ is constructed by gluing
305	  $\binom{r}{d}$ affine charts $U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]$ indexed by
306	  size-$d$ subsets $I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}$. The chart $U^I$
307	  carries the matrix $X^I$ of size $d \times r$ whose $I$-th $d \times d$
308	  minor is the identity, with the remaining $d(r-d)$ entries free
309	  variables (so $U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}$). The
310	  transition maps $\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I$ defined
311	  by $X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I$ on the locus where $\det X^I_J$ is
312	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
313	  $\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}$. The glued
314	  $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}$ is smooth of relative dimension
315	  $d(r-d)$. Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
316	  $\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J$ cut out by $X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0$.
317	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
318	  $\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$ over a DVR $R$ with fraction
319	  field $K$, factoring through some chart $U^I$ via a ring map
320	  $f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K$, choose $J$ minimising the valuation
321	  $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ of the minor; on the chart $U^J$ all entries have
322	  non-negative valuation, so $f$ extends uniquely to $\Spec R$.
323	
324	  The universal quotient $u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
325	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}$ is given on $U^I$ by the matrix $X^I$
326	  and glued by $g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)$. The
327	  determinant line bundle $\det\mathcal{U}$ has transition functions
328	  $\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J$; the global sections
329	  $\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})$ defined by
330	  $\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I$ form a base-point-free linear system separating
331	  points over $\Spec\mathbb{Z}$, giving an embedding into
332	  $\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}$, which is closed by
333	  properness. Representability of $\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)$ is then verified
334	  on the universal pair $(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)$: a quotient
335	  $q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}$ of
336	  rank $d$ on $T$ is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
337	  the columns indexed by $I$ are a basis the matrix of $q$ has the form
338	  $X^I$, defining the classifying morphism $T \to U^I \subset
339	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$. Base change from $\mathbb{Z}$ to a general noetherian
340	  base $S$ with a vector bundle $V$ is direct: trivialise $V$ on an open
341	  cover and glue. For non-trivial $V$ the same construction produces
342	  $\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)$ with Pl\"ucker embedding into
343	  $\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)$.
344	\end{proof}
345	
346	\section{Representability of the Quot scheme}
347	\label{sec:quot_representable}
348	
349	\begin{theorem}[Representability of the Quot functor]
350	  \label{thm:quot_representable}
351	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme}
352	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:grassmannian_scheme,
353	        thm:grassmannian_representable,
354	        thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
355	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Main Existence Theorems"
356	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
357	  % L2220-L2273); cf.\ Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV.
358	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
359	  % "Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $\pi: X\to S$ a projective morphism,
360	  %  and $L$ a relatively very ample line bundle on $X$. Then for any
361	  %  coherent ${\mathcal O}_X$-module $E$ and any polynomial $\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$,
362	  %  the functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ is representable
363	  %  by a projective $S$-scheme $\quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$."
364	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, Theorem (Grothendieck), Theorem
365	  (Altman--Kleiman) (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ Grothendieck, FGA TDTE-IV.}
366	  Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, $\pi : X \to S$ a projective morphism,
367	  $L$ a relatively very ample line bundle on $X$, $E$ a coherent
368	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, and $\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$. Then the
369	  functor $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ of \cref{def:quot_functor} is
370	  representable by a projective $S$-scheme $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$,
371	  equipped with a universal quotient
372	  \[
373	    q^{\mathrm{univ}} :
374	    \pi^*_{\Quot} E \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}^{\mathrm{univ}}
375	    \quad \text{on } X \times_S \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S},
376	  \]
377	  flat over $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ with constant Hilbert polynomial
378	  $\Phi$ on every fiber. The Hilbert scheme is the special case
379	  $E = \mathcal{O}_X$: $\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} = \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}$
380	  represents the functor of $T$-flat closed subschemes
381	  $Y \subset X_T$ with fiberwise Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$.
382	\end{theorem}
383	
384	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: this proof is long (~5 named sub-sections in [Nitsure]
385	% \S 5). Each sub-step is recorded with its own SOURCE QUOTE in the
386	% sub-lemmas below. The structural skeleton of the proof is restated in
387	% project notation in the \begin{proof} block.
388	\begin{proof}
389	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:quot_functor, def:grassmannian_scheme,
390	        thm:grassmannian_representable,
391	        thm:flattening_stratification_exists,
392	        lem:quot_boundedness, lem:quot_alpha_injective,
393	        lem:quot_valuative_criterion}
394	  The proof has four steps; each step is a sub-lemma in the next
395	  sub-sections.
396	  \begin{enumerate}
397	    \item \textbf{(Boundedness, \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}.)} Pick the
398	      Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound $m = m(\mathrm{rank}\,V,
399	      \mathrm{rank}\,W, \Phi)$ uniformly across all fibers (by Mumford's
400	      $m$-regularity theorem applied to $E_s$ and to all coherent
401	      quotients of $E_s$ with Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$). For $r \ge m$
402	      and any $T \to S$ and any $T$-flat quotient
403	      $q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}$ with Hilbert polynomial
404	      $\Phi$, the higher direct images $R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)$
405	      and $R^i (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)$ and $R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)$
406	      vanish for $i \ge 1$ (where $\mathcal{G} = \ker q$), and the
407	      direct images $(\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)$, $(\pi_T)_* E_T(r)$,
408	      $(\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)$ are locally free of ranks fixed by the
409	      data; the canonical maps $(\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)$
410	      are surjective. Cohomology-and-base-change (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology},
411	      Stacks tag 02KH) propagates these properties across $T$-base change.
412	    \item \textbf{(Grassmannian embedding, \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}.)}
413	      Reduce by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} to the case
414	      $X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)$, $E = \pi^* W$ with $V, W$ locally free
415	      on $S$, $L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)$. Fix $r \ge m$.
416	      The Grassmannian embedding
417	      \[
418	        \alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
419	        \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
420	          W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
421	        \right)
422	      \]
423	      sends $\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle$ on $T$ to the locally free
424	      quotient $(\pi_T)_*(q(r)) : (\pi_T)_* E_T(r) \to (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)$.
425	      This is injective on $T$-valued points: from the map $T \to
426	      \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))$ one recovers
427	      the kernel-pull-back $(\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r) \to E_T(r)$,
428	      whose cokernel is $q(r) : E_T(r) \to \mathcal{F}(r)$;
429	      twisting back by $-r$ recovers $q$.
430	    \item \textbf{(Locally closed in the Grassmannian via flattening
431	      stratification.)} Let $T_0 = \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V,
432	      \Phi(r))$ with universal quotient
433	      $f : (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_{T_0} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{J}$
434	      and kernel $\mathcal{K}$. Form the composite
435	      $h : \pi_{T_0}^* \mathcal{K} \hookrightarrow
436	      \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V) = \pi_{T_0}^* (\pi_{T_0})_* E_{T_0}
437	      \twoheadrightarrow E_{T_0}$
438	      and let $q := \mathrm{coker}(h) : E_{T_0} \to \mathcal{F}$. By the
439	      flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
440	      of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}) applied to $\mathcal{F}$
441	      on $X_{T_0}$, there exists a locally closed subscheme
442	      $T_0^\Phi \subseteq T_0$ such that for any $\phi : Y \to T_0$, the
443	      pulled-back cokernel $\mathcal{F}_Y$ is $Y$-flat with fiberwise
444	      Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$ iff $\phi$ factors through $T_0^\Phi$.
445	      The pair $(T_0^\Phi, \text{universal cokernel})$ represents
446	      $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$.
447	    \item \textbf{(Closed, hence projective,
448	      \cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}.)} The functor
449	      $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ satisfies the valuative criterion of
450	      properness with respect to DVRs (Exercise (7) of \S 1, proved
451	      using that flatness over a DVR is torsion-freeness). Combined
452	      with the locally closed embedding of step 3 into the projective
453	      $\mathrm{Gr}_S \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^{\Phi(r)}
454	      (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V))$, this upgrades to a \emph{closed}
455	      embedding. Hence $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ is a projective $S$-scheme.
456	  \end{enumerate}
457	  Finally, by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}, the general case
458	  (arbitrary $X$ projective over $S$ and arbitrary coherent $E$) follows
459	  from the case $X = \mathbb{P}(V)$, $E = \pi^* W$: pick a closed
460	  embedding $X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)$ and write $E$ as a coherent quotient
461	  of $\pi^*(W)(\nu)$ for some vector bundle $W$ on $S$ and integer $\nu$;
462	  the Quot scheme of the original data is then a closed subscheme of the
463	  Quot scheme of the universal data, by the closed-embedding clause of
464	  \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}.
465	\end{proof}
466	
467	\section{Sub-lemmas of the construction}
468	\label{sec:quot_sub_lemmas}
469	
470	\begin{lemma}[Reduction to the universal case]
471	  \label{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}
472	  \uses{def:quot_functor}
473	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Reduction to the case of
474	  % $\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}$"
475	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
476	  % L2294-L2332).
477	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
478	  % "It is enough to prove Theorem \ref{Altman-Kleiman Quot} in the special case
479	  %  that $X = \PP(V)$ and
480	  %  $E= \pi^*(W)$ where $V$ and $W$ are vector bundles on $S$, as
481	  %  a consequence of the next lemma.
482	  %
483	  %  \begin{lemma}
484	  %  (i) Let $\nu$ be any integer.
485	  %  Then tensoring by $L^{\nu}$ gives an isomorphism of
486	  %  functors from $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ to
487	  %  $\Quot_{E(\nu)/X/S}^{\Psi,L}$ where the polynomial
488	  %  $\Psi \in \Q[\lambda]$ is defined by $\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)$.
489	  %
490	  %  (ii) Let $\phi : E \to G$ be a surjective homomorphism of
491	  %  coherent sheaves on $X$. Then the corresponding natural transformation
492	  %  $\Quot_{G/X/S}^{\Phi,L} \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$
493	  %  is a closed embedding.
494	  %  \end{lemma}"
495	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
496	  Let $S$, $X$, $L$, $E$, $\Phi$ be as in \cref{def:quot_functor}.
497	  \begin{enumerate}
498	    \item (Twist-shift.) For $\nu \in \mathbb{Z}$, tensoring by
499	      $L^{\otimes \nu}$ gives an isomorphism of functors
500	      $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \xrightarrow{\sim}
501	      \Quot^{\Psi,L}_{E(\nu)/X/S}$ with $\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)$.
502	    \item (Surjection induces closed embedding.) Given a surjection
503	      $\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G$ of coherent sheaves on $X$, the
504	      induced natural transformation
505	      $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \to \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ is a closed
506	      embedding of functors.
507	  \end{enumerate}
508	  Consequently, if $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\pi^* W / \mathbb{P}(V)/S}$ is
509	  representable for all $V$, $W$ vector bundles on $S$, then so is
510	  $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ for every $X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)$ closed and
511	  every coherent $E$ on $X$ that is a quotient of $\pi^*(W)(\nu)|_X$ for
512	  some $W$ and $\nu$.
513	\end{lemma}
514	
515	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The statement (i) is obvious. The statement (ii) just
516	% says that given any locally noetherian scheme $T$ and a family
517	% $\langle \F,q\rangle \in \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(T)$, there exists
518	% a closed subscheme $T' \subset T$ with the following universal property:
519	% for any locally noetherian scheme $U$ and a morphism $f: U\to T$,
520	% the pulled back homomorphism of ${\mathcal O}_{X_U}$-modules $q_U : E_U \to \F_U$
521	% factors via the pulled back homomorphism $\phi_U : E_U \to G_U$
522	% if and only if $U \to T$ factors via $T'\hra T$.
523	% This is satisfied by taking $T'$ to be the vanishing scheme
524	% for the composite homomorphism $\ker(\phi) \hra  E \stackrel{q}{\to} \F$
525	% of coherent sheaves on $X_T$ (see Remark \ref{vanishing scheme}),
526	% which makes sense here as both $\ker(\phi)$ and $\F$ are coherent
527	% on $X_T$ and $\F$ is flat over $T$."
528	\begin{proof}
529	  \uses{def:quot_functor}
530	  (i) is direct from the definitions: tensoring with $L^{\otimes \nu}$ is
531	  an autoequivalence of coherent sheaves on $X$ (and on every $X_T$),
532	  preserving surjectivity, $T$-flatness, and properness of support, and
533	  shifting the Hilbert polynomial by $\nu$ in the variable.
534	
535	  (ii) Given a family $\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle$ on $T$ in
536	  $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$, the locus where $q$ factors through
537	  $\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G$ is cut out by the vanishing of the
538	  composite $\ker(\phi)|_{X_T} \hookrightarrow E_T \xrightarrow{q}
539	  \mathcal{F}$. Because $\mathcal{F}$ is $T$-flat, the schematic image of
540	  this composite descends to a closed subscheme $T' \subset T$ such that
541	  $f : U \to T$ factors through $T'$ iff $q_U$ factors through $\phi_U$.
542	  Hence $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \hookrightarrow \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$
543	  is a closed embedding of subfunctors.
544	\end{proof}
