546	\begin{lemma}[Uniform Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound]
547	  \label{lem:quot_boundedness}
548	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:hilbert_polynomial}
549	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Use of $m$-Regularity"

550	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
551	  % L2346-L2404). Builds on [Nitsure] \S 2 (Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity)
552	  % and \S 3 (semi-continuity and base-change), cited internally as
553	  % Theorem~"Mumford" and Theorem~"Base-change for flat sheaves".
554	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
555	  % "We consider the sheaf $E = \pi^*(W)$ on $X=\PP(V)$ where
556	  %  $V$ is a vector bundle on $S$,
557	  %  and take $L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)$.
558	  %  For any field $k$ and a $k$-valued point
559	  %  $s$ of $S$, we have an isomorphism
560	  %  $\PP(V)_s \simeq \PP^n_k$ where $n=\rank(V)-1$,
561	  %  and the restricted sheaf $E_s$ on $\PP(V)_s$ is isomorphic to
562	  %  $\oplus^p{\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)_s}$ where $p=\rank(W)$.
563	  %  It follows from Theorem \ref{Mumford}
564	  %  that given any $\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]$, there exists an integer
565	  %  $m$ which depends only on $\rank(V)$, $\rank(W)$ and $\Phi$,
566	  %  such that for any field $k$ and a $k$-valued point $s$ of $S$,
567	  %  the sheaf $E_s$ on $\PP(V)_s$ is $m$-regular, and for any
568	  %  coherent quotient $q: E_s \to \F$ on $\PP(V)_s$ with
569	  %  Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$, the sheaf $\F$ and the kernel sheaf
570	  %  $\G\subset E_s$ of $q$ are both $m$-regular."
571	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of $m$-Regularity" (FGA Explained
572	  Ch.~5).}
573	  Let $X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)$, $E = \pi^* W$ with $V$, $W$ vector bundles on
574	  $S$ of ranks $n+1$, $p$, $L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)$, and
575	  $\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$. There exists an integer
576	  $m = m(n, p, \Phi)$, depending only on $n$, $p$, and $\Phi$, such that
577	  for every geometric point $s$ of $S$ and every coherent quotient
578	  $q : E_s \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}$ with Hilbert polynomial
579	  $\Phi$, the sheaves $E_s$, $\mathcal{F}$, and $\ker q$ on $X_s$ are all
580	  $m$-regular in the sense of Castelnuovo--Mumford. Consequently, for
581	  every $T \to S$ and every $T$-flat quotient
582	  $q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}$ on $X_T$ with Hilbert
583	  polynomial $\Phi$ and every $r \ge m$, the conclusions \textbf{(*)} and
584	  \textbf{(**)} of \cite[\S 5, "Use of $m$-Regularity"]{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
585	  hold: the direct images $(\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)$, $(\pi_T)_* E_T(r)$,
586	  $(\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)$ are locally free of fixed ranks; the
587	  canonical surjections $(\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \twoheadrightarrow (-)(r)$
588	  hold; and the higher direct images $R^i (\pi_T)_* (-)(r)$ vanish for
589	  $i \ge 1$.
590	\end{lemma}
591	
592	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of $m$-Regularity"
593	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L2346-L2404).
594	% The proof invokes the m-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2
595	% (Theorem "Mumford") to extract the uniform bound m, and the
596	% base-change-for-flat-sheaves theorem of [Nitsure] \S 3 (cited as
597	% "Base-change for flat sheaves") to propagate the conclusions across $T$.
598	\begin{proof}
599	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
600	  Each geometric fiber $E_s$ is a trivial bundle on $\mathbb{P}^n_{\kappa(s)}$
601	  of rank $p$, so $E_s$ is $0$-regular. The Castelnuovo--Mumford
602	  $m$-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2 produces, for any fixed Hilbert
603	  polynomial $\Phi$, a uniform integer $m = m(n, p, \Phi)$ above which
604	  every coherent quotient of $E_s$ with Hilbert polynomial $\Phi$ -- and
605	  its kernel -- is $m$-regular. The Castelnuovo Lemma of [Nitsure] \S 2
606	  converts $m$-regularity into vanishing of $H^i((-) (r))$ for $i \ge 1$
607	  and $r \ge m$, and into global generation of $H^0((-)(r))$. To
608	  propagate the fiber-wise statement to families over $T$, apply the
609	  flat-base-change theorem (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}, Stacks
610	  tag 02KH) along $T \to S$: cohomology of flat families commutes with
611	  base change, so the direct images $(\pi_T)_*(-)(r)$ are locally free of
612	  the rank dictated by the fiber Hilbert polynomials, and the canonical
613	  maps $(\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)$ inherit fiberwise
614	  surjectivity.
615	\end{proof}

617	\begin{lemma}[Grassmannian embedding $\alpha$ is injective]
618	  \label{lem:quot_alpha_injective}
619	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:grassmannian_scheme, lem:quot_boundedness}
620	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
621	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
622	  % L2409-L2451).
623	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
624	  % "We now fix a positive integer $r$ such that $r\ge m$.
625	  %  Note that the rank of ${\pi_T}_*\F(r)$ is $\Phi(r)$
626	  %  and $\pi_*E(r) = W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V$.
627	  %  Therefore the surjective homomorphism
628	  %  ${\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$
629	  %  defines an element of the set
630	  %  $\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V , \Phi(r))(T)$.
631	  %  We thus get a morphism of functors
632	  %  $$\alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to
633	  %  \Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))$$
634	  %  It associates to $q: E_T \to \F$ the quotient
635	  %  ${\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$.
636	  %
637	  %  The above morphism $\alpha$ is injective because the quotient
638	  %  $q: E_T \to \F$ can be recovered from
639	  %  ${\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)$
640	  %  as follows."
641	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
642	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
643	  In the universal setup $X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)$, $E = \pi^* W$, fix
644	  $r \ge m$ from \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}. The natural transformation
645	  \[
646	    \alpha :
647	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
648	    \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
649	      W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
650	    \right),
651	    \qquad
652	    \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle \longmapsto
653	    \langle (\pi_T)_*\mathcal{F}(r),\, (\pi_T)_*(q(r))\rangle
654	  \]
655	  is well-defined (the target is locally free of rank $\Phi(r)$, and the
656	  canonical isomorphism $\pi_* E(r) \cong W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S}
657	  \mathrm{Sym}^r V$ gives the source) and \emph{injective} on $T$-points:
658	  the data of the locally free quotient at level $r$ determines
659	  $q : E_T \to \mathcal{F}$.
660	\end{lemma}
661	
662	\begin{proof}
663	  \uses{lem:quot_boundedness}
664	  Suppose $\alpha(T)$ sends both $\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle$ and
665	  $\langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle$ to the same $T$-point of the
666	  Grassmannian. Then the pulled-back kernel
667	  $(\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \to (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)
668	  = \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_T$
669	  is the same. The diagram \textbf{(**)} of
670	  \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} gives a right-exact sequence
671	  \[
672	    (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \xrightarrow{h}
673	    E_T(r) \xrightarrow{q_i(r)} \mathcal{F}_i(r) \to 0,
674	  \]
675	  so $q_i(r)$ is recovered as the cokernel of $h$; twisting by $-r$
676	  recovers $q_i$, giving $q_1 = q_2$ and hence
677	  $\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle$.
678	\end{proof}

680	\begin{lemma}[Valuative criterion of properness for $\Quot$]
681	  \label{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}
682	  \uses{def:quot_functor}
683	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
684	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
685	  % L2510-L2542). Original reference: EGA IV(2) 2.8.1, cited there.
686	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
687	  % "The functor $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}$ satisfies the following valuative
688	  %  criterion for properness over $S$: given any discrete valuation ring
689	  %  $R$ over $S$ with quotient field $K$, the restriction map
690	  %  $$\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R) \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)$$
691	  %  is bijective. This can be seen as follows.
692	  %  Given any coherent quotient
693	  %  $q: E_K \to \F$ on $X_R$ which defines an element $\langle \F, q\rangle$
694	  %  of $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)$.
695	  %  Let $\ov{\F}$ be the image of the composite homomorphism
696	  %  $E_R \to j_*(E_K) \to j_*\F$ where $j : X_K \hra X_R$ is the open
697	  %  inclusion. Let $\ov{q} : E_R\to \ov{\F}$ be the induced surjection.
698	  %  Then we leave it to the reader to verify that
699	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ is an element of $\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R)$
700	  %  which maps to $\langle \F, q\rangle$, and is the unique such element.
701	  %  (Use the basic fact that being flat over a dvr
702	  %  is the same as being torsion-free.)"
703	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
704	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5); EGA IV(2) 2.8.1.}
705	  The functor $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}$ satisfies the valuative criterion
706	  for properness over $S$: for every discrete valuation ring $R$ with
707	  fraction field $K$ and every morphism $\Spec R \to S$, the restriction
708	  map
709	  \[
710	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec R)
711	    \longrightarrow
712	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec K)
713	  \]
714	  is bijective.
715	\end{lemma}
716	
717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:quot_functor}
719	  Given a $\Spec K$-point $\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle$ on $X_K$,
720	  let $j : X_K \hookrightarrow X_R$ be the open inclusion. Define
721	  $\overline{\mathcal{F}}$ as the image of the composite
722	  $E_R \to j_* E_K \to j_* \mathcal{F}_K$, with induced surjection
723	  $\overline{q} : E_R \to \overline{\mathcal{F}}$. The sheaf
724	  $\overline{\mathcal{F}}$ is torsion-free over $R$ (being a subsheaf of
725	  $j_* \mathcal{F}_K$), hence $R$-flat (flatness over a DVR
726	  $=$ torsion-freeness), and restricts on the generic fiber to
727	  $\mathcal{F}_K$. The pair $(\overline{\mathcal{F}}, \overline{q})$ is
728	  the unique extension of $\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle$ to a
729	  $\Spec R$-point (uniqueness: any other extension agrees on the dense
730	  open $X_K \subset X_R$ and is torsion-free, hence equals the image
731	  construction). The Hilbert polynomial is constant on connected
732	  $\Spec R$ by flatness.
733	\end{proof}

735	\section{Cohomology and base change}
736	\label{sec:cohomology_base_change}
737	
738	The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
739	boundedness step (\cref{lem:quot_boundedness}) and the Grassmannian
740	embedding (\cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}). We record the relevant
741	statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it directly.
742	
743	\begin{theorem}[Flat base change of cohomology]
744	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology}
746	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology)
747	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947-L970).
748	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
749	  % "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
750	  %  $$
751	  %  \xymatrix{
752	  %  X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
753	  %  S' \ar[r]^g & S
754	  %  }
755	  %  $$
756	  %  Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
757	  %  with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
758	  %  Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
759	  %  For any $i \geq 0$
760	  %  \begin{enumerate}
761	  %  \item the base change map of
762	  %  Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
763	  %  is an isomorphism
764	  %  $$
765	  %  g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
766	  %  $$
767	  %  \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
768	  %  $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
769	  %  \end{enumerate}"
770	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology).}
771	  Let
772	  \[
773	    \begin{array}{ccc}
774	      X' & \xrightarrow{g'} & X \\
775	      \downarrow f' &  & \downarrow f \\
776	      S' & \xrightarrow{g} & S
777	    \end{array}
778	  \]
779	  be a cartesian diagram of schemes with $g$ flat and $f$ quasi-compact and
780	  quasi-separated. Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent
781	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module with pull-back
782	  $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^* \mathcal{F}$. For every $i \ge 0$:
783	  \begin{enumerate}
784	    \item the base-change map
785	      $g^* R^i f_* \mathcal{F} \to R^i f'_* \mathcal{F}'$ is an isomorphism;
786	    \item if $S = \Spec A$ and $S' = \Spec B$, then $H^i(X, \mathcal{F})
787	      \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
788	  \end{enumerate}
789	\end{theorem}
790	
791	\section{Lean encoding}
792	\label{sec:quot_lean_encoding}
793	
794	The Lean target file is the new
795	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. The construction
796	proceeds in three phases.
797	
798	\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
799	On a finite-type morphism $\pi : X \to S$ with $S$ noetherian and a
800	coherent $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$ with proper support over $S$, the per-fiber
801	Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function $s \mapsto
802	\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$. Mathlib provides
803	\texttt{CategoryTheory.PolynomialModule} and Euler-characteristic
804	infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian schemes; the polynomial
805	property of $m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})$ requires
806	Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
807	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
808	
809	\paragraph{Phase 2: Grassmannian scheme (\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme},
810	\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).} The Grassmannian
811	$\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)$ is encoded as the relative gluing of $\binom{r}{d}$
812	affine charts $U^I$ (each isomorphic to a relative affine space
813	$\mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_S$) via the Pl\"ucker cocycle $\theta_{I,J}$. The
814	gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData}. The
815	universal quotient sheaf $\mathcal{U}$ is glued by the transition cocycle
816	$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}$, and the determinant line bundle
817	$\det \mathcal{U}$ gives the Pl\"ucker closed embedding into
818	$\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)$. The representability statement uses
819	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} (as in
820	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) on the universal pair
821	$(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d), \mathcal{U})$.
822	
823	\paragraph{Phase 3: Quot scheme (\cref{thm:quot_representable}).}
824	The Quot functor is constructed as a locally closed subfunctor of the
825	Grassmannian
826	$\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))$, via:
827	\begin{enumerate}
828	  \item the uniform $m$-regularity bound from
829	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} (input: Castelnuovo--Mumford theorem,
830	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.CastelnuovoMumford} -- project-side sub-build);
831	  \item the Grassmannian embedding $\alpha$ from
832	    \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective};
833	  \item the flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
834	    of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}, A.2.a) applied to the
835	    universal quotient on the Grassmannian, producing the locally closed
836	    stratum $T_0^\Phi$;
837	  \item the valuative-criterion upgrade
838	    (\cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}) to closed embedding, hence
839	    projective $S$-scheme.
840	\end{enumerate}
841	The reduction \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} from the universal
842	case to the general case is encoded as a closed embedding of representable
843	subfunctors via the kernel-vanishing scheme.
844	
845	\paragraph{Cohomology and base change.}
846	\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} is the Mathlib-equivalent of
847	Stacks tag 02KH. Mathlib carries a partial form
848	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.flat\_base\_change}) for affine bases; the
849	full $R^i f_*$ statement is a project-side bridge if not present at the
850	pinned commit.
851	
852	\paragraph{Dependencies summary.}
853	This file directly consumes
854	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, on disk) for relative-spectrum
855	gluing infrastructure and
856	\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} from
857	\cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification} (A.2.a, written this iter)
858	for the flattening stratum. Downstream, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
859	(A.2.c) consumes \cref{thm:quot_representable} via the special case
860	$E = \mathcal{O}_C$ on a smooth proper curve $C/k$, giving the Hilbert
861	scheme $\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot^{\Phi,\mathcal{O}_C(1)}_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}$
862	and its open subscheme $\Div_{C/k}$ of relative effective divisors.
863	
864	\section{Out of scope}
865	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
866	
867	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-scheme existence statement and
868	its proof skeleton via the Grassmannian + flattening-stratification
869	route. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
870	\begin{itemize}

9	\label{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}
10	
11	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.a sub-row of Route A

18	%     smooth-variety setting where the prover can phrase it as

26	% A bonus output is \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}: the Weil-divisor

31	%

35	% \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
36	% \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} (the A.4.b sub-row). The chapter
37	% records the dependency but does not re-prove it.
38	%
39	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean}
40	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
41	% creating it.

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ throughout. By a \emph{variety} over $\bar k$
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over $\bar k$
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over $\bar k$.
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism $\varphi_U \colon U \to Y$ defined on a dense open subset
54	$U \subseteq X$, where two pairs $(U, \varphi_U)$, $(U', \varphi_{U'})$ are equivalent
55	if $\varphi_U$ and $\varphi_{U'}$ agree on $U \cap U'$ (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map $f$
56	is \emph{defined at} a point $x \in X$ if there is some representative $(U, \varphi_U)$
57	with $x \in U$; the set of such $x$ is an open subset $\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X$,
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of $f$.
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of $X$?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on $X$ or $Y$ the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if $Y$ is complete (proper over $\bar k$), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-$1$ point of a normal source $X$ (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if $Y$ is a group variety, the difference map
75	    $\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}$ on $X \times X$ has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For $Y$ an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-$1$ extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's $\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}$ API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
94	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
95	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
96	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let $V$ and $W$
97	  % be varieties over $k$\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
98	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
99	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map $\varphi$ is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
100	  % $v$ of $V$ if $v \in U$ for some $(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi$. The set $U_1$ of
101	  % $v$ at which $\varphi$ is defined is open, and there is a regular map
102	  % $\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W$ such that $(U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi$ --- clearly,
103	  % $U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U$ and we can define $\varphi_1$ to be
104	  % the regular map such that $\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U$ for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
105	  % \varphi$."
106	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
107	  Let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y$ be a rational map between varieties over $\bar k$.
108	  The \emph{domain of definition} of $f$ is the open set
109	  \[
110	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
111	  \]
112	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of $f$. The
113	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of $f$ is the closed complement
114	  \[
115	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
116	  \]
117	  The map $f$ is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of $X$) if and only if $Z(f) = \emptyset$.
118	
119	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
120	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the

1	\chapter{Auslander--Buchsbaum}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.b sub-row of Route A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the corollary ``regular local ring
8	% $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay'' that A.4.a (the codim-1 extension proof for
9	% Albanese morphisms out of a regular projective surface) consumes. Per
10	% STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
11	% startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the
12	% Albanese geometry and lives entirely under \texttt{RingTheory.*}. The
13	% target Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}
14	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
15	% creating it. Note that Mathlib already houses parts of the depth /
16	% projective-dimension API
17	% (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth.*},
18	% \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence},
19	% \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ProjectiveResolution}); a Plan-Agent audit
20	% at the pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) should confirm what is present
21	% before the prover writes the file, since a fully Mathlib-backed
22	% Auslander--Buchsbaum would collapse this chapter to a re-export.
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface $S$ over a field --- such as $\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1$
28	or the product $C \times C$ of a smooth projective curve $C$ with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension $2$. To extend a rational map
30	$f : S \dashrightarrow A$ from $S$ to an abelian variety across a codimension-$2$ closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring $\mathcal{O}_{S,x}$ at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} $2$, hence a rational function with no codimension-$1$ poles on a neighbourhood of $x$
33	extends across $x$. The depth $\geq 2$ inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring $R$ one has $\text{pd}_R(R) = 0$,
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension $d$ admits an $R$-regular sequence of length $d$ generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains $\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)$, i.e.\ $R$ is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its $\text{Ext}$ reformulation
47	    (Definition~\ref{def:depth} and Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_via_ext}),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~\ref{def:projective_dimension}),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~\ref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}), and
54	  \item the corollary regular $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~\ref{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~\ref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	  [Depth of a module]
66	  \label{def:depth}
67	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
68	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
69	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
70	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
71	  % "Let $R$ be a ring, and $I \subset R$ an ideal. Let $M$ be a finite $R$-module.
72	  %  The {\it $I$-depth} of $M$, denoted $\text{depth}_I(M)$, is defined as follows:
73	  %  \begin{enumerate}
74	  %  \item if $IM \not = M$, then $\text{depth}_I(M)$ is the supremum in
75	  %  $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}$ of the lengths of $M$-regular sequences in $I$,
76	  %  \item if $IM = M$ we set $\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty$.
77	  %  \end{enumerate}
78	  %  If $(R, \mathfrak m)$ is local we call $\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)$ simply
79	  %  the {\it depth} of $M$."
80	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
81	  Let $R$ be a commutative ring, let $I \subseteq R$ be an ideal, and let $M$ be a finitely
82	  generated $R$-module. The \emph{$I$-depth} of $M$, written $\text{depth}_I(M)$, is the
83	  supremum in $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}$ of the lengths of $M$-regular sequences contained
84	  in $I$, provided $IM \neq M$; if $IM = M$ we set $\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty$. When
85	  $(R, \mathfrak{m})$ is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
86	  $\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)$ and call this simply the
87	  \emph{depth} of $M$. The depth of the ring $R$ itself is $\text{depth}(R)$, the depth
88	  of $R$ as a module over itself.
89	\end{definition}
90	
91	Recall that a sequence $f_1, \dots, f_r \in R$ is called \emph{$M$-regular} if
92	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0$ and for each $i$, the element $f_i$ is a non-zero-divisor on
93	$M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M$. The empty sequence is regular on $M$ iff $M \neq 0$ (it is
94	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention $\text{depth}(0) = \infty$
95	above is the usual one).
96	
97	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
98	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the $\text{Ext}$ characterisation (used to establish an
99	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
100

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
7	% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
8	% codim-$\geq 2$ extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),
9	% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary
10	% ``regular local $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay''
11	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ to a regular morphism $J \to A$,
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
25	% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved
26	% here (see "Notes for Plan Agent" in the writer report).
27	%
28	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}
29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, a smooth variety $X$ over $\bar k$ (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over $\bar k$ that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety $A$ over $\bar k$ (in particular $A$ is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in $\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k$). A rational map
40	$f \colon X \dashrightarrow A$ is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism $\varphi_U \colon U \to A$ defined on a dense open subset
42	$U \subseteq X$, two such pairs $(U, \varphi_U)$ and $(U', \varphi_{U'})$ being equivalent
43	if $\varphi_U$ and $\varphi_{U'}$ agree on $U \cap U'$.
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
49	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
50	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
51	  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
52	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
53	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
54	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
55	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map $\alpha\colon V--\to A$ from a nonsingular
56	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of $V$."
57	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
58	
59	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, let $A$ be
60	  an abelian variety over $\bar k$, and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow A$ be a rational
61	  map. Then $f$ is defined on the whole of $X$: there is a unique regular morphism
62	  $\widetilde f \colon X \to A$ whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
63	  set of definition $U \subseteq X$ of $f$ agrees with $\varphi_U$.
64	\end{theorem}
65	
66	The uniqueness clause is immediate from $X$ being reduced and separated: two regular
67	morphisms $X \to A$ that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
68	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-$\geq 2$
69	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
70	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
71	
72	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
73	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
74	\begin{proof}
75	  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
76	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
77	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-$\geq 2$ extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
78	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
79	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
80	  pure-codimension-$1$ structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
81	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
82	
83	  Concretely: let $U \subseteq X$ be a maximal open set of definition of $f$, and let
84	  $Z := X \setminus U$ be its complement.
85	  \begin{enumerate}
86	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
87	      to codim $\geq 2$), applied with the normal variety $X$ and the complete variety
88	      $A$, we have $\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2$.
89	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-$1$
90	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety $A$), the closed subset
91	      $Z$ is either empty or of pure codimension $1$ in $X$.
92	    \item Both conditions together force $Z = \emptyset$: a non-empty pure-codimension-$1$
93	      closed subset has codimension exactly $1$, contradicting $\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2$.
94	  \end{enumerate}
95	  Therefore $U = X$ and $f$ admits a regular representative on all of $X$, which is the
96	  required regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X \to A$. Uniqueness is the standard
97	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
98	\end{proof}
99	
100	\begin{remark}
101	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
102	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
103	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
104	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-$1$ step
105	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
106	  $\mathcal O_{X, Z}$ of each codimension-$1$ point $Z$ --- works because $X$ is normal
107	  and $A$ is complete) and a codim-$\geq 2$ step (depth-$\geq 2$ at codim-$\geq 2$ points,
108	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
109	  depth-$\geq 2$ input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
110	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
111	  for a regular local ring $(\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)$ of Krull dimension $d$, the
112	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to $M = R$
113	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
114	  $\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d$, so in particular
115	  $\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2$ whenever $\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2$.
116	  Concretely, when $X$ is a smooth surface (e.g.\ $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ or
117	  $C \times C$ for a smooth projective curve $C$), every closed point $x$ is regular of
118	  dimension $2$, so $\mathcal O_{X, x}$ is Cohen--Macaulay of depth $2$; sections of
119	  $\mathcal O_X$ on $U = X \setminus \{x\}$ extend uniquely across $x$, and the same is
120	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of $\mathcal O_A$ along $f$.
121	
122	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
123	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-$\geq 2$ half. The dependency
124	  $\uses{thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}$ is recorded on
125	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
126	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
127	\end{remark}
128	
129	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
130	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
131	
132	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
133	\begin{itemize}
134	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-$\geq 2$ extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
135	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-$\geq 2$ extension
136	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
137	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
138	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
139	    $\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A$ and
140	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
141	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
142	    on $A$ (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of $X$ (so divisors and
143	    DVRs at codim-$1$ points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
144	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
145	    has a characteristic restriction.
146	\end{itemize}
147	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
148	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
149	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
150	
151	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
152	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
153	
154	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
155	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$. Milne's proof of
156	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
157	morphism $\psi \colon J \to A$ factoring a given pointed regular morphism
158	$\varphi \colon C \to A$ through the canonical $f_P \colon C \to J$ as follows. The
159	symmetric power $C^{(g)}$ admits a regular morphism $\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A$
160	defined by
161	\[
162	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
163	\]
164	and a birational morphism $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J$ (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
165	seeks a morphism $J \to A$ making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
166	$C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ followed by descent to $J$; the rational map
167	$C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
168	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because $C^{(g)}$ is
169	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and $A$ is an abelian variety. Without
170	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
171	shortcut ($J \cong J^\vee$) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
172	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
173	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
174	
175	\section{Lean encoding}
176	\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}
177	
178	The Lean target file is the new
179	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}. The single declaration
180	this chapter targets is the theorem
181	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}, with signature (in informal
182	notation):
183	\[
184	  \texttt{X : Scheme} \;\to\; \texttt{[Smooth X over $\bar k$]} \;\to\;
185	  \texttt{A : AbelianVariety $\bar k$} \;\to\;
186	  (f : \texttt{X.RationalMap A}) \;\to\;
187	  \exists ! \, \widetilde f \colon X \to A, \;\; \widetilde f \;\text{extends}\; f.
188	\]
189	The proof body imports \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s exports and
190	\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}'s exports from
191	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean} and
192	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} respectively, and combines them
193	in the three-step pattern above (codim $\geq 2$ from A.4.a + pure-codim $1$ from A.4.a
194	$\Rightarrow$ empty indeterminacy locus).
195	
196	The Mathlib namespace target is
197	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap} (already housed in
198	\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap} at the project's pinned commit;
199	see \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, where the same namespace hosts the
200	order-at-a-prime-divisor map). The extension is therefore a method on the existing
201	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure rather than a free-standing predicate, allowing
202	downstream consumers in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP} to call it as
203	\texttt{f.extend\_to\_av}.
204	
205	No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a two-line combination of A.4.a and
206	A.4.b. The chapter is therefore inexpensive on the prover side (the LOC budget is dominated
207	by A.4.a and A.4.b themselves); its purpose is to record the precise statement and the
208	exact dependency wiring so that A.4.d can consume a single named lemma.
209	
210	\section{Out of scope}
211	\label{sec:thm32_out_of_scope}
212	
213	This chapter packages \emph{only} the theorem
214	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne~3.2) and the immediate proof combining its
215	two inputs. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of scope here:
216	\begin{itemize}
217	  \item \textbf{The codim-$\geq 2$ extension theorem (Milne Theorem~3.1) itself.}
218	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
219	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; this chapter only consumes it.
220	  \item \textbf{The pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy lemma (Milne Lemma~3.3) itself.}
221	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
222	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; this chapter only consumes it.
223	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and its Cohen--Macaulay corollary
224	    (Milne~$\equiv$ Stacks 090V / 00OD).} Proved in
225	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
226	    \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this chapter only consumes them (indirectly,
227	    inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see \cref{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}).
228	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
229	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the special cases ``rational map
230	    from a product of nonsingular varieties to an AV vanishing on the axes is constant'',
231	    ``rational map between AVs = homomorphism + translation'', ``birationally equivalent
232	    AVs are isomorphic'', ``every $\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A$ is constant'',
233	    ``every rational map from a unirational variety to an AV is constant'') is downstream
234	    of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not needed by A.4 in the project's
235	    current routing: the genus-$0$ base case
236	    \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route} closes
237	    via the proven Rigidity Lemma + $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut without invoking any of
238	    these. They are recorded as off-path in
239	    \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}.
240	  \item \textbf{The Albanese universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ (Milne Proposition~6.1).}
241	    Consumed by A.4.d, proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}; this chapter only
242	    provides the technical extension lemma that A.4.d invokes when promoting
243	    $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ to $C^{(g)} \to A$.
244	  \item \textbf{Generalisations beyond an algebraically closed base field.} Milne's
245	    Theorem~3.2 is stated for varieties over an algebraically closed field; the
246	    project's positive-genus arm runs over $\bar k$ throughout (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}).
247	    Descent of the extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would
248	    require additional Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
249	\end{itemize}

2	
3	\chapter{The line bundle $\mathcal O_C(P)$ and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of $\mathscr L(D)$); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
23	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group $\Div(C)$, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z$, and the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~$0$
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); the global-sections
35	calculation $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a smooth
36	proper genus-$0$ curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(P)$ associated to a closed point $P \in C$
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its $\bar k$-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$
50	in the genus-$0$ specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that $C$ admits a non-constant rational function $f$
52	having at most a simple pole at $P$ and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	$C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ whose degree-$1$ identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$
64	% to vanish $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ from the degree count
65	% $\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0$. Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-$0$ specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$ is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-$0$ hypothesis
75	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0$ and the skyscraper
76	% identity $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$. The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-$g$
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
82	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus $g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point is the one of
94	\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order $\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	\Cref{def:principal_divisor} and \Cref{def:order_at_point}.
98	
99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	\begin{definition}
102	
103	[Line bundle of a closed point]
104	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
106	  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor,
107	        def:order_at_point}
108	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
109	  % sheaf $\mathscr L(D)$ associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
110	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
111	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
112	  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
113	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
114	  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
115	  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
116	  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, and $f_i^{-1}$ and
117	  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call
118	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ the \emph{sheaf associated to $D$}."
119	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let $X$ be a scheme. Then:
120	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor $D$, $\mathscr L(D)$ is an invertible
121	  % sheaf on $X$.}"
122	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
123	  $\mathscr L(D)$ and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
124	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
125	  closed point. Let $\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}$ denote the
126	  maximal ideal of the local ring at $P$; by smoothness of $C$,
127	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation ring and $\mathfrak m_P$
128	  is generated by any uniformizer $f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$ (an
129	  element of $\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2$). The Cartier
130	  divisor associated to the closed point $P$ is the effective
131	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer $f_P$ on an open
132	  affine neighbourhood of $P$, and by the unit $1$ on the complement
133	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. The \emph{line bundle of the closed point $P$},
134	  denoted $\mathcal O_C(P)$, is the locally free $\mathcal O_C$-module
135	  of rank $1$ defined by Hartshorne's construction $\mathscr L(D)$
136	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
137	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
138	  $\mathcal K_C$ (the constant sheaf of the function field $K(C)$ on the
139	  integral scheme $C$) that is generated by $f_P^{-1} \in K(C)$ on a
140	  neighbourhood of $P$ and by $1 \in K(C)$ on the complement
141	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
142	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
143	  uniformizer $f_P$ (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
144	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}$, so they generate the same
145	  $\mathcal O_C$-submodule of $\mathcal K_C$).
146	
147	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
148	  $\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C$ denote the ideal sheaf of the
149	  closed point $P$ (locally generated by the uniformizer $f_P$ near
150	  $P$, and equal to $\mathcal O_C$ on the complement). Then
151	  $\mathcal I_P$ is the line bundle $\mathcal O_C(-P)$ associated to the
152	  Weil divisor $-[P]$, and $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
153	  $\mathcal O_C$-linear dual:
154	  \[
155	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
156	    \mathcal O_C)
157	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
158	  \]
159	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
160	  ``subsheaf of $\mathcal K_C$'' description above is the one used in
161	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
162	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
163	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
164	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
165	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
166	
167	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
168	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
169	  scheme $C$, a point $P : C$, and a closedness witness
170	  $\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}$ (the same input shape as
171	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), and returns an invertible
172	  $\mathcal O_C$-module $\mathcal O_C(P)$. The well-definedness as an
173	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
174	  smoothness of $C$ (delivering the DVR stalk at $P$) and the standard
175	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
176	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
177	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
178	  closed plus dimension-$1$-integral delivers $\codim_C \{P\} = 1$
179	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
180	\end{definition}
181	
182	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
183	
184	\begin{lemma}
185	
186	[Global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as rational functions with a
187	controlled pole]
188	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
190	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:order_at_point,
191	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
192	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
193	  % divisors linearly equivalent to $D_0$ are the divisors of zeros of
194	  % global sections of $\mathscr L(D_0)$, with the explicit
195	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
196	  % $f$ satisfying $(f) \geq -D_0$) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let $X$ be a nonsingular
199	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field $k$. Let
200	  % $D_0$ be a divisor on $X$ and let $\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)$

300	  \mathcal O_C$ of coherent rank-$1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
301	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
302	  $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}$ has trivial stalk. The
303	  quotient is a torsion coherent $\mathcal O_{C'}$-module of generic rank
304	  $\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0$, hence is itself torsion-free (a
305	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
306	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
307	  \mathcal O_{C'}$ and $\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
308	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
309	
310	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
311	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
312	  non-constant finite morphism $\varphi \colon C \to C'$ together with the
313	  degree-$1$ hypothesis $\deg(\varphi) = 1$ and produces an isomorphism
314	  $C \cong C'$ in the slice category $\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}$.
315	  The proof body threads the Mathlib API
316	  \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
317	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
318	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
319	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
320	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
321	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
322	\end{proof}
323	
324	\section{Headline: genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
325	
326	\begin{theorem}
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$]
330	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
331	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
332	  \uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections,
333	        lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso,
334	        cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero,
335	        thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero,
336	        def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
337	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S1 Example~1.3.5 (genus-zero classification;
338	  % "rational $\iff g = 0$" + "rational curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ for
339	  % complete nonsingular curves") (read from
340	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314, doc p.~297).
341	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
342	  % if it is birational to $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this
343	  % chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve $X$ is
344	  % rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now using
345	  % (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We
346	  % already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
347	  % suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0. Let $P, Q$ be two distinct
348	  % points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor $D = P - Q$.
349	  % Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$,
350	  % and so we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by
351	  % (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies
352	  % $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
353	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
354	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field, and let $C$ be a smooth
355	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$.
356	  Then there exists an isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes
357	  \[
358	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
359	  \]
360	  Concretely, the Lean signature
361	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
362	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
363	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
364	  \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
365	  \Cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} is its consumer-side restatement,
366	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
370	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
371	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
372	% "We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots"). The present
373	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
374	% (linear-system construction $C \to \mathbb P^1$ via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
375	% ([P]))$, degree-$1$ identification, isomorphism).
376	\begin{proof}
377	  \uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections,
378	        lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso,
379	        cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero,
380	        thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero,
381	        def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
382	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ the smooth proper
383	  geometrically irreducible curve $C$ has closed points, all of which are
384	  $\bar k$-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
385	  $\bar k$ is a finite extension of $\bar k$, hence is $\bar k$ itself by
386	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such $\bar k$-rational point $P \in C$;
387	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
388	
389	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$.}
390	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
391	  (\Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
392	  effective divisor $[P]$ of degree $1$, we obtain
393	  $\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2$. The constant section
394	  $1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ accounts for a
395	  one-dimensional $\bar k$-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
396	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
397	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
398	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
399	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
400	
401	  \emph{Step 2: morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$ via the linear system $(1, f)$.}
402	  The pair $(1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2$ generates the
403	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$ at every point of $C$: at points
404	  $Q \in C \setminus \{P\}$ the section $1$ does not vanish in
405	  $\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}$, and at the marked point
406	  $P$ the section $f$ does not vanish in $\mathcal O_C([P])_P$ (because $f$
407	  has a pole of order exactly $1$ at $P$ in the rational-function
408	  picture: $f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k$ with $\div(f)_\infty = [P]$).
409	  Therefore by \Cref{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
410	  exists a unique morphism
411	  \[
412	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
413	  \]
414	  with $\varphi^* x_0 = 1$ and $\varphi^* x_1 = f$ (so the pullback of
415	  the standard ratio coordinate $u = x_1 / x_0$ on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
416	  \{\infty\}$ is $f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
417	  $t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f$ is the local parameter $1/f$ at $P$).
418	
419	  \emph{Step 3: $\deg(\varphi) = 1$ via the pole divisor of $f$.} The
420	  morphism $\varphi$ is non-constant (since $f$ is non-constant as a
421	  rational function on $C$, the pullback $\varphi^* u = f$ takes
422	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant $\varphi$). By
423	  \Cref{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to $\varphi$,
424	  \[
425	    \deg(\varphi)
426	    \;=\;
427	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
428	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
429	  \]
430	  We compute the pole divisor of $f$ as a rational function on $C$:
431	  since $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$ with $f$ non-constant and
432	  $\mathcal O_C([P])$ the line bundle of the effective divisor $[P]$, the
433	  rational-function $f$ has pole divisor $(f)_\infty = [P]$ (the
434	  unique pole of order $1$ at $P$, no other poles; this is the standard
435	  identification of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$ with the rational functions
436	  $g \in K(C)$ satisfying $\div(g) + D \geq 0$, specialised to
437	  $D = [P]$). Hence $\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}$ with multiplicity~$1$
438	  and
439	  \[
440	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
441	  \]
442	
443	  \emph{Step 4: $\varphi$ is an isomorphism.} By
444	  \Cref{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant degree-$1$
445	  morphism $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ (smooth proper
446	  geometrically irreducible curves over $\bar k$ on both sides), $\varphi$
447	  is an isomorphism of $\bar k$-schemes.
448	
449	  This produces the required isomorphism $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$
450	  closing the headline target
451	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
452	
453	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
454	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
455	  $P, Q \in C$ and the degree-zero divisor $D = P - Q$, deriving $P \sim Q$
456	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that $C$ is rational; combined with I.6.12
457	  this yields $C \cong \mathbb P^1$. The present proof restates the same
458	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
459	  construction: instead of going through the $P \sim Q$ statement and
460	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
461	  $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1$ from the two sections $(1, f)$ of
462	  $\mathcal O_C([P])$ given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
463	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
464	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero $\Rightarrow$
465	  $\ell([P]) = 2$ $\Rightarrow$ non-constant $f$ $\Rightarrow$ degree-$1$
466	  map to $\mathbb P^1$ $\Rightarrow$ iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
467	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
468	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
469	\end{proof}

144	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
145	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
146	  affine local pieces $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ for each affine open $U \subseteq X$ and
147	  open-immersion transition data
148	  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$ for $U \subseteq U'$
149	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
150	  $\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)$; quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
151	  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$, so each transition is an
152	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
153	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.

219	  because morphisms of schemes and $\mathcal{O}$-algebra maps both glue under open covers.

224	  base-change identification $F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}$
225	  (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_base_change}). Each $F_i \hookrightarrow F$ is an open
226	  subfunctor (pull back $U_i$ along $f$). The $F_i$ jointly cover $F$ since the $U_i$
227	  cover $X$. The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,

329:    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ be the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$. As the identity component of a $k$-group scheme locally of finite type, $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is geometrically irreducible; for $C$ smooth proper of genus $g$, it is moreover a smooth proper $k$-group scheme of dimension $g$. Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map $\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$ is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is the kernel of the degree map $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k$, which is locally constant). 29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve $C$ over $k$ (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in $C$ and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a $k$-scheme $T$; the relative curve over $T$ is
37	$C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T$, with $\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C$ the
38	companion projection. Both $\pi_T$ and $\pi_C$ are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and $C/k$ is flat since $k$ is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group $\Pic(C \times_k T)$ of the total space, and the
43	Picard group $\Pic(T)$ of the base. The pullback functor $\pi_T^*$ sends a line
44	bundle on $T$ to its base-change line bundle on $C \times_k T$; the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
